1:
"But a certain man named Ananias, with Sapphira his wife, sold a possession," The witnesses note the entrance of hypocrisy into the church, set against the generosity of Barnabas. Gill observes the contrast: the account of Ananias "follows upon that of Barnabas, and is opposed to it"; his name signified "the grace of the Lord" or "the Lord's gracious one," yet "he was not so, but a graceless person." Gill notes "there are hypocrites among men of the greatest names and characters," and that Ananias may even have been one of the hundred and twenty, having "great gifts" yet "no grace." The witnesses note Ananias and his wife selling a possession, in seeming imitation of the church's liberality. The witnesses note the hypocrite entering the church.

The witnesses note Ananias and Sapphira's sale — hypocrisy entering the church amid its generosity.

So the witnesses make the verse the entrance of hypocrisy into the church. The lesson the expositors draw is the sad reality of hypocrisy among professors, set in dark contrast to the true generosity that preceded. The witnesses note the contrast Gill marks: the account of Ananias follows immediately upon that of Barnabas (Acts 4:36-37), who had generously sold his land and laid the price at the apostles' feet; and Ananias is set in opposition to him — the false generosity against the true. The expositors observe the irony of the name: "Ananias" signified "the grace of the Lord" or "the Lord's gracious one"; yet the man was graceless, a hypocrite — proof that names and outward profession are no sign of grace. The witnesses press the sobering truth Gill draws: there are hypocrites among men of the greatest names and characters; Ananias may even have been one of the hundred and twenty, possessing great gifts yet no grace — a warning that gifts, profession, and place in the church are no proof of a renewed heart. The expositors draw the lesson on hypocrisy in the church: even in the purest age of the church, amid the outpouring of the Spirit and the generosity of the saints, hypocrisy crept in; Ananias and Sapphira the first recorded hypocrites in the Christian church, a warning that the visible church always contains the false among the true. The witnesses note the occasion of their sin: the church's practice of selling possessions for the common good (Acts 4:34-35) gave occasion for their hypocrisy; they would have the credit of generosity without its cost, the praise of the liberal while keeping back part for themselves. The expositors observe the motive implied: Ananias and Sapphira coveted the reputation of the generous, seeking the honor Barnabas received, but unwilling to make the full sacrifice; their sin springing from vanity and covetousness joined. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned that hypocrisy may enter even the truest fellowship, that names and gifts are no proof of grace, and that the desire for reputation without sincerity is the seed of Ananias's sin. The verse thus records the entrance of Ananias and Sapphira — selling a possession in false imitation of the church's generosity — teaching the sad reality of hypocrisy among professors, set in contrast to true liberality.
2:
"And kept back part of the price, his wife also being privy to it, and brought a certain part, and laid it at the apostles' feet." The witnesses note the deceitful holding back and the wife's complicity. Gill notes Ananias "reserved it for his own use, after he had given out that he sold it for the service of the church"; the deceit being that he professed to give all while keeping back part. Lightfoot discusses the word "kept back" (enosphisato, embezzled, as Achan, Joshua 7). The witnesses note the couple's joint scheme — keeping back part of the price, the wife privy to it, and bringing only a portion while pretending it was the whole. The witnesses note the deceitful pretense.

The witnesses note the deceit: keeping back part while pretending to give all — the wife a partner in the fraud.

So the witnesses make the verse the deceitful holding back. The lesson the expositors draw is the nature of their sin — not the keeping of their own money, but the lie of pretending to give all while keeping back part. The witnesses note the act: "kept back part of the price... and brought a certain part, and laid it at the apostles' feet" — Ananias reserved a portion for himself, yet brought the rest as though it were the whole, pretending to the full generosity of Barnabas while secretly retaining part. The expositors observe the word Lightfoot marks — "kept back" (the same Greek used of Achan's embezzling, Joshua 7) — denoting a fraudulent, deceitful withholding; not an honest reservation, but a sly keeping-back joined to a false profession. The witnesses press the heart of the sin Gill names: the deceit lay in the pretense — Ananias gave out that he sold the land for the church's service and brought the whole, while in truth he kept back part; the lie, not the keeping, the sin. The expositors draw the lesson, anticipating Peter's words (v.4): the money was Ananias's own, and he was free to keep all or part; his sin was not in retaining his property, but in lying about it — professing to give all while keeping back part, seeking the credit of full generosity by a fraud. The witnesses note the wife's complicity: "his wife also being privy to it" — Sapphira was a partner in the scheme, knowing and consenting to the deceit; the sin a joint conspiracy of husband and wife, both guilty, both to share the judgment. The expositors observe the aggravation of a shared sin: that husband and wife, who should have helped each other to good, agreed together in a fraud; their union, meant for mutual godliness, perverted to mutual deceit. The witnesses draw the lesson on the danger of partial sacrifice joined to pretended completeness: the desire to seem more generous than one is, to have the reputation of full devotion while holding back, is the very sin of Ananias; hypocrisy in giving as offensive to God as in any other duty. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned against the lie of pretended devotion, the keeping back joined to the false profession, and the complicity that draws others into sin. The verse thus records the deceitful holding back — part kept, the whole pretended, the wife privy — teaching that the sin was the lie, not the keeping, and the danger of seeking the credit of generosity by fraud.
3:
"But Peter said, Ananias, why hath Satan filled thine heart to lie to the Holy Ghost, and to keep back part of the price of the land?" The witnesses note Peter's Spirit-given discernment and the charge of lying to the Holy Ghost. Gill notes Peter knew Ananias's secret "by divine revelation, or by a spirit of discerning, such as Elisha had" (2 Kings 5:26). Lightfoot draws the striking contrast of Peter's own restoration: "he who by a lie, yea, even perjury itself, had denied his Master, doth such severe execution upon another for a lie." The witnesses note Peter charging Ananias with letting Satan fill his heart to lie to the Holy Ghost — the sin against the Spirit who dwelt in the apostles. The witnesses note the charge of lying to the Holy Ghost.

The witnesses note Peter's discernment and his charge — Satan filling the heart to lie to the Holy Ghost.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's charge of lying to the Holy Ghost. The lesson the expositors draw is the Spirit-given discernment of the apostle, the agency of Satan in the sin, and the dreadful nature of lying to the Holy Ghost. The witnesses note Peter's supernatural knowledge: he knew the secret fraud "by divine revelation, or by a spirit of discerning" (Gill), as Elisha knew Gehazi's secret theft (2 Kings 5:26); the Spirit revealing to Peter what Ananias had hidden — a proof of the discerning power given the apostles. The expositors observe Lightfoot's striking contrast: Peter, who had himself denied his Master with a lie and an oath, yet now executes such severe judgment on another's lie — the restored sinner made the instrument of judgment, his own fall behind him, his discernment and authority restored. The witnesses press the agency of Satan: "why hath Satan filled thine heart" — the sin was prompted by Satan, who filled Ananias's heart with the design of deceit; yet (as with Judas) the devil's filling did not excuse, for Ananias gave place to him, his own covetousness opening the door. The expositors draw the lesson on the source and the responsibility of sin: Satan fills the heart that is open to him; the tempter prompts, but the sinner consents; Ananias is charged with the sin, though Satan filled his heart, for he yielded to the temptation. The witnesses note the great charge: "to lie to the Holy Ghost" — Ananias's lie was not merely to men, but to the Holy Ghost who dwelt in the apostles and the church; the attempt to deceive the apostles was an attempt to deceive the Spirit who spoke and acted through them; a sin against God himself (as v.4 makes plain). The expositors observe the deity of the Spirit implied: to lie to the Holy Ghost is to lie to God (v.4); so the Holy Ghost is God — a witness, in passing, to the Spirit's divine personhood and deity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the discernment given the apostles, the agency of Satan in sin yet the sinner's responsibility, and the dreadful nature of lying to the Holy Ghost, who is God. The verse thus records Peter's charge — Satan filling the heart to lie to the Holy Ghost — teaching the Spirit-given discernment, the agency of Satan, and the deity of the Spirit against whom Ananias sinned.
4:
"Whiles it remained, was it not thine own? and after it was sold, was it not in thine own power? why hast thou conceived this thing in thine heart? thou hast not lied unto men, but unto God." The witnesses note the voluntariness of the giving and the climactic truth that the Spirit is God. Gill stresses the giving was free: "selling of possessions at this time was a voluntary thing... a pure act of liberality, not enjoined by the apostles; every man was left to his liberty." Barnes agrees: the obligation was not imposed; those who gave, gave voluntarily. The witnesses note the climax: "thou hast not lied unto men, but unto God" — the lie to the Holy Ghost (v.3) is here called a lie to God, proving the Spirit's deity. The witnesses note the voluntary giving and the lie to God.

The witnesses note the giving was free — and the climax: lying to the Holy Ghost is lying to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the voluntariness of the giving and the deity of the Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is that the early sharing was free, not compelled, and that to lie to the Holy Ghost is to lie to God — a proof of the Spirit's deity. The witnesses note Peter's establishing of Ananias's freedom: "whiles it remained, was it not thine own? and after it was sold, was it not in thine own power?" — before the sale the land was his to keep or sell; after the sale the money was his to give or retain; he was under no compulsion. Gill and Barnes stress that the selling of possessions was voluntary, a pure act of liberality, not enjoined by the apostles; the early community of goods was free, not forced — against any notion of compulsory communism. The expositors draw the lesson on the freedom of Christian giving: Ananias might have kept all his money without sin; the apostles imposed no law of common ownership; the giving was the free expression of love, each as he purposed in his heart (2 Corinthians 9:7) — so that Ananias's sin was not in keeping his money, but in lying about it. The witnesses press the heart of the sin: "why hast thou conceived this thing in thine heart?" — the sin was conceived in the heart, a deliberate, premeditated deceit; not a sudden fall, but a planned fraud. The expositors note the climactic truth, the doctrinal heart of the passage: "thou hast not lied unto men, but unto God" — the lie to the Holy Ghost (v.3) is here called a lie to God; so the Holy Ghost is God. The witnesses press the proof of the Spirit's deity: Peter, in two successive verses, calls the same lie a lie "to the Holy Ghost" (v.3) and a lie "unto God" (v.4); the Holy Ghost and God are thus identified — the Spirit is a divine Person, God himself; a clear scriptural witness to the deity of the Holy Spirit against all who would make him a mere influence or creature. The expositors observe the aggravation of the sin: Ananias thought to deceive men, but his lie reached to God, who cannot be deceived and who searches the heart; the folly and the guilt of attempting to deceive the omniscient God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the freedom of Christian giving, the sin of the lying heart, and the deity of the Holy Spirit, to lie to whom is to lie to God. The verse thus records the voluntary giving and the lie to God — was it not thine own? thou hast lied to God — teaching the freedom of Christian liberality and the deity of the Spirit against whom Ananias sinned.
5:
"And Ananias hearing these words fell down, and gave up the ghost: and great fear came on all them that heard these things." The witnesses note the sudden death of Ananias as a divine judgment. Barnes notes the charge "came upon him unexpectedly and terribly, like a bolt of thunder"; and that "gave up the ghost" simply means "he expired, he died." The witnesses note that the death was a direct, immediate judgment of God — not by Peter's hand or word, but by the stroke of God; and the great fear it produced among all who heard. The witnesses note the death of Ananias and the fear it caused. The witnesses note the divine judgment.

The witnesses note Ananias's sudden death — an immediate judgment of God — and the great fear it stirred.

So the witnesses make the verse the death of Ananias as a divine judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is the severity of God against hypocrisy in his church, and the holy fear it produced. The witnesses note the suddenness: "Ananias hearing these words fell down, and gave up the ghost" — at Peter's words exposing his sin, Ananias fell down dead on the spot; Barnes notes the charge came upon him unexpectedly and terribly, like a thunderbolt, and that "gave up the ghost" means simply that he died, expired. The expositors press that this was a direct judgment of God: not by Peter's hand, nor by any natural cause, but by the immediate stroke of God — the Lord himself smiting the hypocrite dead; a signal and extraordinary judgment, marking the heinousness of the sin. The witnesses observe the reason for so severe a judgment at this time: in the infancy of the church, when its purity and the awe of God's presence were to be established, God made a fearful example of hypocrisy — that the church might be kept pure, and that all might learn the danger of mocking God; a judgment fitted to the critical hour. The expositors draw the lesson on God's hatred of hypocrisy: the sin of Ananias, seeming small to men (a lie about money), was so offensive to God that he smote it with death; hypocrisy in the church, the attempt to deceive God, a sin God abhors and, here, signally punished. The witnesses note the effect: "great fear came on all them that heard these things" — the judgment produced a deep, holy fear among all who witnessed or heard of it; the awe of God's presence and justice falling on the church. The expositors observe the salutary purpose of this fear: it purified the church, deterring hypocrisy and impressing all with the holiness and justice of God; the fear a wholesome effect, guarding the church's purity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the severity of God against hypocrisy, the immediate judgment on Ananias, and the holy fear that should attend the sense of God's presence and justice. The verse thus records the death of Ananias and the fear it caused — falling down dead, great fear on all — teaching the severity of God against hypocrisy and the holy fear it rightly produced.
6:
"And the young men arose, wound him up, and carried him out, and buried him." The witnesses note the prompt and decent burial of Ananias. Gill notes "the young men" were "the younger brethren of the church, who were robust and strong, and fit for the following service," rising at once, unwilling that the awful spectacle should lie long. Lightfoot notes they bound up the dead in what cloths they could and carried him out to burial, the burying-places being outside the city. The witnesses note the young men promptly wrapping the body and carrying it out for burial. The witnesses note the burial of Ananias.

The witnesses note the young men promptly burying Ananias — the swift, decent disposal of the judged hypocrite.

So the witnesses make the verse the burial of Ananias. The lesson the expositors draw is the prompt and orderly burial, and the solemn aftermath of the judgment. The witnesses note the actors: "the young men" — Gill identifies them as the younger, stronger brethren of the church, fit for the service of carrying out and burying the dead; rising at once, unwilling that the awful spectacle of the judged hypocrite should lie long before the assembly. The expositors observe the promptness: the young men arose immediately, wound up the body (Lightfoot notes, in what burial cloths they could find at hand), carried it out, and buried it; the whole done swiftly, the Jewish custom being to bury without delay, and the burying-places lying outside the city. The witnesses note the orderliness and decency: though Ananias died under judgment, his body was decently wrapped and buried; the church did not cast it out with contempt, but gave it burial — order and decency maintained even in the solemn aftermath of judgment. The expositors draw the lesson on the solemnity of the scene: the swift removal and burial of the dead hypocrite, in the sight of the assembly, deepened the awe; the judgment not lingered over, but its solemn lesson left on every heart; the church proceeding with grave order. The witnesses observe the providence in the timing: the burial took some three hours (v.7), during which Sapphira, ignorant of her husband's death, would come in — the interval setting the stage for the second judgment; the burial and the wife's arrival providentially ordered. The expositors note the example of the young men's service: the ready, willing service of the younger brethren in a solemn and unpleasant duty; the church's order providing for every need, the young serving the body as the elders served the word. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the prompt, decent burial of the judged hypocrite, the orderly proceeding of the church, and the providence that ordered the interval before Sapphira's coming. The verse thus records the burial of Ananias — the young men wrapping and burying him — teaching the prompt, decent disposal of the dead and the solemn, orderly aftermath of the judgment.
7:
"And it was about the space of three hours after, when his wife, not knowing what was done, came in." The witnesses note Sapphira's arrival in ignorance of her husband's death. Barnes notes that, "as Sapphira had been no less guilty than her husband, so it was ordered in the Providence of God that the same judgment should come upon both." Gill and Lightfoot account for the three hours by the distance to the burial-place. The witnesses note Sapphira coming in after three hours, not knowing what had befallen Ananias — the stage set for her own testing and judgment. The witnesses note Sapphira's ignorant arrival.

The witnesses note Sapphira coming in unaware — the providential interval setting the stage for her testing.

So the witnesses make the verse Sapphira's arrival in ignorance. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that ordered her coming, and the equal justice that would fall on the equally guilty. The witnesses note the interval: "about the space of three hours after" — time enough for Ananias's burial (Gill and Lightfoot account for the length by the distance to the burying-place outside the city); and in that interval Sapphira came in, "not knowing what was done" — ignorant of her husband's death and burial. The expositors observe the providence in her ignorance: that she came in unaware, before any could tell her, set the stage for her own testing — she would be questioned without knowing the fate of her lie, given the same opportunity her husband had to confess or persist; the providence ordering the scene for the trial of her heart. The witnesses note Barnes's point on the equal justice: Sapphira had been no less guilty than Ananias — privy to the scheme, a partner in the fraud (v.2); and so it was ordered by God's providence that the same judgment should fall on both; the equal sin met with equal punishment. The expositors draw the lesson on the impartiality of God's justice: God is no respecter of persons; the wife's complicity made her as guilty as the husband, and the same judgment came on both; partnership in sin brings partnership in punishment. The witnesses observe the testing Sapphira would face: she came in ignorant, and would be given the chance to tell the truth or to persist in the lie (v.8); the providence not entrapping her, but giving her, like Ananias, the opportunity to repent of the deceit — her judgment following her own free persistence in the lie. The expositors note the solemnity of the unfolding scene: the church, still under the awe of Ananias's death, would witness the testing and judgment of his wife; the second act of the solemn drama, deepening the lesson. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence ordering the scene, the equal justice on the equally guilty, and the testing that gave Sapphira, like her husband, the chance to confess. The verse thus records Sapphira's ignorant arrival — three hours after, not knowing — teaching the providence that set the stage and the equal justice prepared for the equally guilty.
8:
"And Peter answered unto her, Tell me whether ye sold the land for so much? And she said, Yea, for so much." The witnesses note Peter's testing question and Sapphira's confirming lie. Barnes explains the nature of the falsehood: it was true that they had received that sum, but they had received more; "it is as really a falsehood to deceive in this manner as to affirm that they received much more than they did. Falsehood consists in making an erroneous representation of a thing for the purpose of deceiving." The witnesses note Peter giving Sapphira the chance to tell the truth, and her persisting in the lie — confirming the sum as the whole. The witnesses note Sapphira's deliberate lie.

The witnesses note Peter's testing question and Sapphira's lie — persisting in the deceit when offered the truth.

So the witnesses make the verse Sapphira's confirming lie. The lesson the expositors draw is the nature of the falsehood and the deliberate persistence in deceit. The witnesses note Peter's question: "Tell me whether ye sold the land for so much?" — Peter, holding up the sum Ananias had brought, asks Sapphira whether that was the price; giving her the opportunity to tell the truth, to confess the holding-back, to escape her husband's fate. The expositors observe the test this offered: Sapphira was not entrapped, but given a fair chance to be honest; the question a door to confession — had she told the truth, she might have been spared; the lie was her own free choice. The witnesses note her answer: "Yea, for so much" — she persisted in the lie, confirming the part as the whole; choosing deceit when offered the truth, sealing her guilt by her own deliberate falsehood. The expositors press Barnes's analysis of the falsehood: it was literally true that they had received that sum — but they had received more; and to represent the part as the whole, for the purpose of deceiving, is as real a falsehood as to claim they received what they did not; falsehood consists in any erroneous representation made to deceive. The witnesses draw the lesson on the subtlety of lying: Sapphira did not state an open untruth (they had received that much), but used a true statement to convey a false impression — the lie of misleading, of half-truth, as guilty as the boldest falsehood; a warning against the deceit that hides in technical truth. The expositors observe the deliberateness of her sin: she had three hours to reflect, came in composed, and lied without hesitation when questioned; her sin not a sudden slip, but a deliberate, considered persistence in the fraud; her guilt the deeper for its deliberateness. The witnesses note the lost opportunity: Peter's question was a last chance for confession, mercifully given; Sapphira's lie threw it away, choosing to deceive rather than confess; the judgment that followed her own free choice. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the nature of the lie (the misleading half-truth), the mercy of the chance to confess, and the deliberate persistence that sealed Sapphira's guilt. The verse thus records Sapphira's confirming lie — yea, for so much — teaching the nature of the deceitful half-truth and the deliberate persistence in falsehood that rejected the offered chance to confess.
9:
"Then Peter said unto her, How is it that ye have agreed together to tempt the Spirit of the Lord? behold, the feet of them which have buried thy husband are at the door, and shall carry thee out." The witnesses note the charge of conspiracy and tempting the Spirit. Gill draws the contrast: for husband and wife to agree in what is good is commendable, "but to agree in a fraud, in a lie, is very dreadful." Barnes explains "tempt the Spirit" — they conspired "to try, or to endeavour to impose on, the Spirit of the Lord," acting as if the Spirit could not detect their crime. The witnesses note Peter charging the couple's conspiracy to tempt the Spirit, and foretelling Sapphira's immediate death. The witnesses note the charge of tempting the Spirit.

The witnesses note Peter's charge: conspiring to tempt the Spirit — and the foretelling of Sapphira's death.

So the witnesses make the verse the charge of tempting the Spirit and the sentence on Sapphira. The lesson the expositors draw is the dreadfulness of conspiring in sin and of tempting the Spirit of the Lord. The witnesses note the charge of conspiracy: "how is it that ye have agreed together" — Peter exposes the joint scheme of husband and wife; and Gill draws the contrast: for a husband and wife to agree in what is good is commendable, but to agree in a fraud and a lie is dreadful — the union meant for mutual godliness perverted to a partnership in deceit. The expositors press the aggravation of the conspiracy: the sin was not a solitary fall, but a deliberate agreement, the two consulting and consenting together to deceive; the shared sin doubling the guilt, each confirming the other in evil. The witnesses note the great charge: "to tempt the Spirit of the Lord" — Barnes explains they conspired to try, to put to the test, the Spirit of the Lord, acting as if the Spirit could not detect their crime; a daring presumption, challenging the omniscience of the Spirit, supposing they could deceive God. The expositors draw the lesson on tempting the Spirit: to "tempt" the Spirit is to try whether he will detect and punish sin, to presume on his patience or doubt his knowledge; Ananias and Sapphira, lying to the Spirit, tempted him — daring him, as it were, to expose them; a fearful presumption against the all-knowing God. The witnesses observe again the deity of the Spirit: "the Spirit of the Lord" whom they tempted is the God against whom they sinned (v.4); the lie, the conspiracy, the tempting, all against the divine Spirit — the passage repeatedly affirming his Godhead. The witnesses note the sentence: "the feet of them which have buried thy husband are at the door, and shall carry thee out" — Peter foretells Sapphira's immediate death; the young men, returning from Ananias's burial, would carry her out also; the judgment certain and at hand. The expositors observe the solemn foreknowledge: Peter, by the Spirit, knew and announced her doom — not as a curse of his own, but as the declared judgment of God; the same judgment as her husband's, for the same sin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the dreadfulness of conspiring in sin, the presumption of tempting the Spirit, and the certainty of the judgment on deliberate, shared deceit. The verse thus records the charge of tempting the Spirit and the sentence on Sapphira — agreed to tempt the Spirit, the feet at the door — teaching the guilt of conspiracy in sin and the fearful presumption of tempting the Spirit of the Lord.
10:
"Then fell she down straightway at his feet, and yielded up the ghost: and the young men came in, and found her dead, and, carrying her forth, buried her by her husband." The witnesses note Sapphira's death and the debated question of the couple's eternal state. Clarke stresses it was "by an immediate judgment of God," not by Peter's words or prayers nor through shame; and weighs their eternal state: "I think their sin was what the apostle (1 John 5:16) calls a sin unto death; a sin which must be punished with temporal death... while mercy" might be extended to the soul. The witnesses note Sapphira falling dead like her husband, and being buried beside him. The witnesses note Sapphira's death and burial.

The witnesses note Sapphira's death by God's stroke — and the debated hope for the couple's souls.

So the witnesses make the verse Sapphira's death and the question of their eternal state. The lesson the expositors draw is the certain divine judgment on her sin, and the debated but not despairing question of the couple's souls. The witnesses note the immediacy: "fell she down straightway at his feet, and yielded up the ghost" — at Peter's word, Sapphira fell dead on the spot, as her husband had; Clarke stresses it was by an immediate judgment of God, not by Peter's words or prayers, nor through shame or remorse, but by the direct stroke of the Lord. The expositors observe the equal justice fulfilled: the same judgment fell on the wife as on the husband, the equally guilty equally punished; and the young men, returning from Ananias's burial, carried her forth and buried her beside him — the two united in sin, in death, and in the grave. The witnesses note the debated question Clarke raises: the eternal state of Ananias and Sapphira. Clarke judges their sin "a sin unto death" (1 John 5:16) — a sin punished with temporal death, the death of the body; yet he holds that mercy might still have been extended to their souls; their bodies smitten, their souls perhaps spared. The expositors weigh the matter with caution: some, with Clarke, hope that the temporal judgment was the whole of their punishment, their souls saved as by fire (1 Corinthians 3:15); others fear their hypocrisy marked them as unregenerate, and their death the seal of their doom; the Scripture does not decide, and the witnesses do not presume beyond it. The witnesses note the proper restraint: the passage records the temporal judgment certainly, but leaves the eternal state undeclared; the believer is not to pronounce confidently where Scripture is silent, but may hope that the severity was the chastening of God's own, or fear it was the judgment of the false. The expositors draw the lesson on the severity and the mystery: the certain lesson is God's hatred of hypocrisy and the reality of his judgment; the uncertain question of the soul left to God, who judges righteously. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the immediate judgment of God, the equal justice on the couple, and the cautious, undespairing reserve concerning their eternal state. The verse thus records Sapphira's death and burial — fallen dead, buried by her husband — teaching the certain divine judgment on her sin and the debated, cautiously held question of the couple's souls.
11:
"And great fear came upon all the church, and upon as many as heard these things." The witnesses note the holy fear that purified the church. Clarke draws the lesson: "this judgment answered the end for which it was inflicted; a deeply religious fear occupied every mind, and hypocrisy and deception were banished from this holy assembly." He notes here "a native specimen of a New Testament Church" — called by the gospel, grafted in by baptism, animated by love, united in fellowship, "and disciplined by the exemplary punishment of hypocrites." The witnesses note the great fear coming on the whole church and all who heard — the wholesome effect of the judgment. The witnesses note the purifying fear.

The witnesses note the holy fear on the church — the judgment purifying the assembly and banishing hypocrisy.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy fear that purified the church. The lesson the expositors draw is the wholesome effect of the judgment in producing a deep, purifying fear of God. The witnesses note the spread of the fear: "great fear came upon all the church, and upon as many as heard these things" — not only the church present at the judgment, but all who heard of it, far and near, were filled with awe; the fear of God falling widely through the report of the judgment. The expositors observe Clarke's point on the purpose answered: the judgment achieved its end — a deeply religious fear occupied every mind, and hypocrisy and deception were banished from the holy assembly; the church purified, the false deterred, the awe of God's holiness impressed on all. The witnesses press the wholesome nature of this fear: it was not a slavish terror that drove men from God, but a holy reverence that purified the church — the fear of God that is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 9:10), keeping the assembly pure and the members sincere. The expositors draw the lesson on the discipline of the church: Clarke marks here a specimen of a New Testament church — called by the gospel, grafted in by baptism, animated by love, united in fellowship, and disciplined by the exemplary punishment of hypocrites; the discipline of the false an essential mark of a pure church, here exercised by God's own hand. The witnesses observe the balance: the church was animated by love (the generous sharing of the saints) and yet guarded by fear (the judgment of the hypocrites); love and holy fear together keeping the church pure and warm — neither a cold severity nor a lax indulgence, but love disciplined by reverence. The expositors note the effect on the church's witness: the purity and awe of the church, far from hindering its growth, advanced it (as the next verses show); the holy fear commending the church to the sincere and deterring the false. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the purifying fear that the judgment produced, the specimen of a true church (called, baptized, loving, united, disciplined), and the holy reverence that should guard the purity of God's people. The verse thus records the great fear on the church — purifying the assembly, banishing hypocrisy — teaching the wholesome effect of the judgment and the holy fear that marks and guards a true church.
12:
"And by the hands of the apostles were many signs and wonders wrought among the people; (and they were all with one accord in Solomon's porch." The witnesses note the return to miracles of mercy after the judgment. Matthew Henry marks the transition: "many miracles of mercy for one of judgment; now the gospel power returned to its proper channel, which is that of mercy and grace. God had come out of his place to punish, but now returns to his place, to his mercy-seat again." Gill notes the signs were wrought "by their means, or by them as instruments." The witnesses note the apostles working many miracles of mercy, the church gathered with one accord in Solomon's porch. The witnesses note the return to miracles of mercy.

The witnesses note the apostles' many miracles of mercy — the gospel power returning to its channel of grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the return to miracles of mercy. The lesson the expositors draw is that, after the one miracle of judgment, God returned to his proper work of mercy and grace, the apostles working many wonders of healing. The witnesses note the abundance of miracles: "by the hands of the apostles were many signs and wonders wrought among the people" — the apostles, as God's instruments (Gill notes), wrought many miraculous cures and wonders; the power of God working mightily through them. The expositors observe Matthew Henry's beautiful marking of the transition: many miracles of mercy for the one of judgment (the death of Ananias and Sapphira); the gospel power, which had come out to punish, now returned to its proper channel — that of mercy and grace; God, having come out of his place to judge, returns to his place, to his mercy-seat again. The witnesses press the lesson on God's delight in mercy: judgment is God's "strange work" (Isaiah 28:21), to which he resorts only when needful; his proper and delighted work is mercy; and after the necessary judgment on hypocrisy, he returns to the abundant outpouring of grace — the many miracles of mercy his natural and characteristic working. The expositors draw the lesson on the balance of judgment and mercy: the church saw both — the severity of God against hypocrisy and the abundance of his mercy in healing; the one judgment establishing reverence, the many mercies displaying grace; both needful, both from the same God. The witnesses note the unity of the church: "they were all with one accord in Solomon's porch" — the believers gathered together in unity, in the public place of the temple where the apostles taught and wrought their wonders; the church one in heart, meeting openly, the miracles done in public view. The expositors observe the boldness of the open gathering: in Solomon's porch, the very temple, the apostles continued their work publicly — undeterred by the opposition, gathered with one accord, the miracles confirming their witness before all. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the return to miracles of mercy after judgment, God's delight in grace over judgment, and the unity and boldness of the gathered church. The verse thus records the apostles' many miracles of mercy — wrought among the people, the church in Solomon's porch — teaching the return of the gospel power to its channel of mercy and grace after the one judgment.
13:
"And of the rest durst no man join himself to them: but the people magnified them." The witnesses note the awe that restrained the insincere and the esteem of the people. Gill weighs who "the rest" were — discussing whether the profane and persecuting (who durst not approach), or the insincere who feared to join hypocritically after the fate of Ananias. The witnesses note the double effect: the judgment and the holy awe deterred the insincere from joining the church lightly, while the people at large held the apostles in high esteem. The witnesses note the awe restraining the false and the people's esteem. The witnesses note the restraining awe.

The witnesses note the awe restraining the insincere from joining — while the people magnified the apostles.

So the witnesses make the verse the awe that restrained the insincere and the people's esteem. The lesson the expositors draw is the double effect of the church's purity and power — deterring the false from joining lightly, yet drawing the esteem of the people. The witnesses note the restraint: "of the rest durst no man join himself to them" — the awe produced by the judgment and the miracles kept the insincere from joining the church; Gill weighs who "the rest" were, but the sense is that the false, the merely curious, and the hypocritical durst not attach themselves to so holy and so powerful a fellowship, fearing the fate of Ananias. The expositors observe the wholesome effect: the purity and the awe of the church deterred the insincere from joining — none dared to come in hypocritically, as Ananias had, lest they too be exposed and judged; the church kept pure by the very fear that the judgment produced. The witnesses press the lesson on the church's holiness: a church marked by holiness and the manifest presence of God will repel the false even as it draws the true; the insincere shrink from a fellowship where hypocrisy is judged; the purity of the church its safeguard against the influx of the unworthy. The expositors note the contrast with the people's regard: "but the people magnified them" — while the insincere feared to join, the people at large held the apostles in high esteem, honoring them for their power and holiness; the church respected and admired, though not lightly joined. The witnesses observe the balance of the two effects: the awe restrained the false from a hypocritical joining, yet the esteem of the people prepared the way for the true to come (the next verse, "believers were the more added"); the holiness that deterred the false drew the sincere. The expositors draw the lesson on the right kind of church growth: not the careless influx of the unconverted, but the addition of true believers, drawn by the church's power and purity; the awe a filter, keeping out the false while the true were added. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the holiness that restrains the insincere, the esteem of the people, and the purity that draws the true while repelling the false. The verse thus records the awe restraining the insincere and the people's esteem — none durst join lightly, yet the people magnified them — teaching the double effect of the church's purity and power.
14:
"And believers were the more added to the Lord, multitudes both of men and women.)" The witnesses note the growth of the church in true believers. Gill notes the case of Ananias and Sapphira "was so far from hindering persons from coming into the church, that there were greater additions made to it than before, even of such as were true believers in Christ." The witnesses note that, despite (and even through) the awe of the judgment, true believers — multitudes of both men and women — were added to the Lord and his church; the addition not of the careless but of the genuine. The witnesses note the addition of true believers. The witnesses note the church's growth.

The witnesses note multitudes of true believers added to the Lord — the church growing through, not despite, its purity.

So the witnesses make the verse the growth of the church in true believers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the church grew, not despite its purity and the awe of judgment, but through them — true believers added in multitudes. The witnesses note the addition: "believers were the more added to the Lord, multitudes both of men and women" — great numbers, of both sexes, were added to the Lord and joined to the church; the church growing abundantly even after, and in connection with, the solemn judgment. The expositors observe Gill's point on the surprising effect: the case of Ananias and Sapphira, far from hindering people from coming into the church, was followed by greater additions than before — and additions of true believers, not the careless or hypocritical; the judgment that deterred the false drew the sincere. The witnesses press the lesson on the kind of growth: the added were "believers... added to the Lord" — genuine converts, joined first to Christ by faith and then to the church; the growth not a mere swelling of numbers, but the addition of real believers; quality and reality, not mere quantity. The expositors draw the lesson on the relation of purity and growth: the awe that restrained the insincere (v.13) did not stop the church's growth, but purified it — the false kept out, the true brought in; the holiness of the church its attraction to the sincere, the very purity that repelled the hypocrite drawing the genuine seeker. The witnesses observe the providence in this: God overruled the judgment to advance the church — the fear it produced purifying the church and commending it to the sincere, so that the church grew in holiness and number together; the purity and the growth advancing hand in hand. The expositors note the inclusion of "both of men and women": the gospel drawing both sexes, the church embracing all; the multitudes added comprising men and women alike, the breadth of the gospel's reach. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the growth of the church in true believers, the purity that drew the sincere, and the providence that made the judgment serve the church's increase. The verse thus records the addition of multitudes of true believers — added to the Lord, men and women — teaching that the church grew through its purity, the awe drawing the sincere even as it repelled the false.
15:
"Insomuch that they brought forth the sick into the streets, and laid them on beds and couches, that at the least the shadow of Peter passing by might overshadow some of them." The witnesses note the people's eagerness and the question of Peter's shadow. Gill notes the sick were brought "into the streets" or "in every street," waiting for the apostles. Barnes notes the word for "beds" denotes "the soft and valuable beds on which the rich commonly lay" — the rich as well as the poor were laid out; and observes carefully that of those healed (v.16) it is "expressly affirmed," but "of those who were so laid as that the shadow of Peter might pass over them, there is no such affirmation." The witnesses note the people's faith in laying the sick for Peter's shadow to fall on them. The witnesses note the eager faith.

The witnesses note the people laying the sick for Peter's shadow — their eager faith, the cures carefully attested.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's eagerness and the question of Peter's shadow. The lesson the expositors draw is the great faith and eagerness of the people, and the careful manner in which the text speaks of the cures. The witnesses note the eager bringing of the sick: "they brought forth the sick into the streets, and laid them on beds and couches" — the people, hearing of the apostles' miracles, brought their sick into the streets (Gill notes, into every street) and laid them out, the rich on valuable beds and the poor on couches (Barnes notes the word for beds denotes the soft beds of the rich), waiting for the apostles to pass. The expositors observe the object of their faith: "that at the least the shadow of Peter passing by might overshadow some of them" — they hoped that even Peter's shadow, falling on the sick, might heal them; a great, if somewhat untaught, faith, expecting healing from the least contact with the apostle. The witnesses note the careful reading Barnes presses: of the sick brought from the cities round about (v.16), it is "expressly affirmed" that they were healed; but of those laid in the streets that Peter's shadow might overshadow them, "there is no such affirmation" — the text records the people's faith and expectation, but does not directly state that the shadow healed them. The expositors weigh the matter with care: some take it that the shadow did heal, God honoring the people's faith (as the hem of Christ's garment, Matthew 9:21, and the handkerchiefs from Paul, Acts 19:12); others, with Barnes, note that the text affirms only the people's expectation, not the cure by the shadow, and would not build doctrine on what is not stated. The witnesses observe that whichever reading is held, the verse certainly displays the great faith and eagerness of the people and the mighty reputation of the apostles' power; and that God may well have honored such faith, as he honored the touch of Christ's garment. The expositors draw the lesson on faith and its objects: the people's faith, though perhaps mingled with ignorance, was real and eager; and God often honors even imperfect faith that earnestly seeks his power. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the eager faith of the people, the careful reading of the text, and the readiness of God to honor faith that seeks his power. The verse thus records the people laying the sick for Peter's shadow — their eager faith, the cures carefully spoken of — teaching the great faith and eagerness of the people and the careful manner of the inspired account.
16:
"There came also a multitude out of the cities round about unto Jerusalem, bringing sick folks, and them which were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed every one." The witnesses note the spread of the apostles' fame and the certain, complete cures. Barnes notes the multitude was "attracted by the fame of Peter's miracles, as the people formerly had been by the miracles of the Lord Jesus"; the "unclean spirits" the demon-possessed; and of these it is "expressly affirmed... they were healed." The witnesses note the crowds from the surrounding cities bringing their sick and the demon-possessed, all of whom were healed — the complete, certain cures attesting the apostles' power. The witnesses note the complete cures. The witnesses note the spreading fame.

The witnesses note multitudes from the cities round about — their sick and possessed all healed, the cures certain and complete.

So the witnesses make the verse the spread of the apostles' fame and the complete cures. The lesson the expositors draw is the wide reach of the apostles' power and the certain, complete healing that attested the gospel. The witnesses note the spreading fame: "there came also a multitude out of the cities round about unto Jerusalem" — the report of the apostles' miracles spread beyond the city to the surrounding towns, drawing crowds; Barnes notes they were attracted by the fame of Peter's miracles, as the people had once been by Christ's. The expositors observe the burdens they brought: "bringing sick folks, and them which were vexed with unclean spirits" — both the sick (of diseases) and the demon-possessed (vexed, troubled, tormented by unclean spirits); the apostles' power extending over both bodily disease and the powers of darkness. The witnesses press the certainty and completeness of the cures: "they were healed every one" — Barnes marks that of these it is expressly affirmed they were healed; not some, but every one; the cures certain, complete, and universal — none failing, all made whole. The expositors draw the lesson on the power that attended the apostles: the same power Christ exercised now worked through the apostles — healing all diseases and casting out all unclean spirits; the gospel confirmed by signs that none could deny, the apostles continuing the works of their Master (John 14:12). The witnesses observe the contrast Barnes implies with the shadow (v.15): there the text records the people's expectation; here it affirms the cures expressly — the inspired writer careful to state plainly what was certainly done, the complete healing of all who came from the cities. The expositors note the advance of the gospel through these signs: the wide fame, the crowds, the complete cures, all advancing the gospel and confirming the apostles' witness; the miracles drawing multitudes to hear and believe. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the wide reach of the apostles' power, the certain and complete cures, and the signs that confirmed the gospel and continued the works of Christ. The verse thus records the multitudes from the cities and the complete cures — their sick and possessed all healed — teaching the wide reach of the apostles' power and the certain, complete healing that attested the gospel.
17:
"Then the high priest rose up, and all they that were with him, (which is the sect of the Sadducees,) and were filled with indignation," The witnesses note the renewed opposition of the Sadducees, moved by jealousy. Chrysostom describes them as "roused, being excited at the things taking place," filled with indignation, assaulting the apostles "more vigorously." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the word is "jealousy" (zēlou) — "in itself only warmth, ardour, zeal, but for a bad cause or from a bad motive, jealousy." The witnesses note the high priest and the Sadducees, filled with indignation and jealousy at the apostles' success, rising to oppose them. The witnesses note the jealous opposition. The witnesses note the Sadducees' indignation.

The witnesses note the Sadducees rising in jealous indignation — opposing the apostles' growing success.

So the witnesses make the verse the renewed opposition of the Sadducees. The lesson the expositors draw is the jealousy that moved the enemies of the gospel against its growing success. The witnesses note the opponents: "the high priest... and all they that were with him, which is the sect of the Sadducees" — the high priest and his party, the Sadducees, who denied the resurrection (Acts 23:8) and were especially provoked by the apostles' preaching of the risen Christ. The expositors observe their state: "were filled with indignation" — Chrysostom describes them as roused and excited by the things taking place, assaulting the apostles more vigorously than before; their indignation kindled by the apostles' success and the multitudes drawn to them. The witnesses press the motive Robertson marks: the word is "jealousy" (zēlou) — in itself only warmth or zeal, but here, for a bad cause and from a bad motive, jealousy; the Sadducees jealous of the apostles' influence, envious of their success, galled by the people's esteem. The expositors draw the lesson on the root of the opposition: it was jealousy — the envy of those who saw their own authority eclipsed and the people drawn away to the apostles; the enemies of the gospel moved not by zeal for truth but by envy of its success; "for envy they had delivered" Christ (Matthew 27:18), and for envy they now opposed his apostles. The witnesses observe the special offense to the Sadducees: the apostles preached the resurrection of Christ, the very doctrine the Sadducees denied; the preaching both refuting their error and undermining their authority; their indignation the deeper for the doctrine that condemned them. The expositors note the pattern of opposition: the success of the gospel provokes the jealousy of its enemies; the more the gospel advances, the more its opponents are stirred; the apostles' very success the occasion of the renewed assault. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the jealousy that moved the enemies of the gospel, the envy that opposes its success, and the special offense of the resurrection to those who deny it. The verse thus records the Sadducees rising in jealous indignation — filled with envy at the apostles' success — teaching the jealousy that moves the enemies of the gospel against its advance.
18:
"And laid their hands on the apostles, and put them in the common prison." The witnesses note the second arrest and its severity. Ellicott notes that the addition of "common" or "public" perhaps indicates a greater severity of treatment — the apostles "not merely kept in custody, but dealt with as common criminals, compelled to herd with ruffians and robbers and murderers." The grammatical-historical Robertson confirms the "jealousy" that drove the arrest. The witnesses note the apostles seized and put in the common (public) prison — treated as common criminals, the second and harsher arrest. The witnesses note the second arrest. The witnesses note the apostles imprisoned.

The witnesses note the second arrest — the apostles laid in the common prison, treated as common criminals.

So the witnesses make the verse the second arrest of the apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is the escalating opposition and the indignity heaped on the apostles, treated as common criminals. The witnesses note the act: "laid their hands on the apostles, and put them in the common prison" — the Sadducees, moved by jealousy, seized the apostles (now the whole company, not Peter and John only) and imprisoned them; the opposition escalating from threats (Acts 4:21) to imprisonment. The expositors observe the severity Ellicott marks: the prison is called the "common" or "public" prison, perhaps indicating greater severity — the apostles not merely held in custody, but dealt with as common criminals, compelled to herd with ruffians, robbers, and murderers; the indignity of being treated as malefactors. The witnesses press the escalation of the opposition: at the first arrest, Peter and John were held overnight and released with threats (Acts 4); now the whole company is seized and put in the common prison — the enemies, their threats having failed, resorting to harsher measures; the opposition mounting as the gospel advances. The expositors draw the lesson on the cost of faithful witness: the apostles, for preaching Christ and working miracles of mercy, are imprisoned as criminals; the world repaying their good with persecution, their healing with imprisonment; the servants treated as their Master was. The witnesses observe the indignity and the injustice: the apostles, who had done only good — healing the sick, casting out demons, preaching life — are treated as common felons; the injustice of the world's opposition, condemning the benefactors of men. The expositors note the providence even in the imprisonment: the arrest would be overruled (the next verse) for the deliverance and the further testimony of the apostles; the enemies' assault serving, in God's hand, to advance the gospel and display his power. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the escalating opposition, the indignity heaped on the faithful, and the providence that overrules persecution for the gospel's advance. The verse thus records the second arrest — the apostles laid in the common prison — teaching the escalating opposition and the indignity of the apostles treated as common criminals for their faithful witness.
19:
"But the angel of the Lord by night opened the prison doors, and brought them forth, and said," The witnesses note the angelic deliverance of the apostles. Gill notes it was "a good angel, an elect angel, one of those ministering spirits sent to minister to the heirs of promise," excelling in strength. Barnes notes the case "was evidently a miracle," an angel employed for this special purpose. Chrysostom observes the deliverance was "both for the encouragement of the disciples, and for the benefit and instruction of the others." The witnesses note an angel opening the prison by night and bringing the apostles out — a miraculous deliverance. The witnesses note the angelic deliverance. The witnesses note the prison opened by night.

The witnesses note the angel opening the prison by night — the miraculous deliverance of the apostles.

So the witnesses make the verse the angelic deliverance. The lesson the expositors draw is the ministry of angels in delivering God's servants, and the purposes God served by the miracle. The witnesses note the deliverer: "the angel of the Lord" — Gill notes a good and elect angel, one of the ministering spirits sent to serve the heirs of salvation (Hebrews 1:14), excelling in strength; sent by God to deliver the imprisoned apostles. The expositors observe the manner: "by night opened the prison doors, and brought them forth" — the angel, by night, opened the locked and guarded prison and led the apostles out; Barnes notes this was evidently a miracle, an angel employed for the special purpose of their deliverance. The witnesses press the ministry of angels to God's people: angels are sent to minister to the heirs of salvation, to guard, deliver, and serve them; here an angel delivers the apostles from prison, a striking instance of the angelic care that watches over God's servants. The expositors draw the lesson on God's power over the enemies' devices: the prison that men had shut, God's angel opened; the bars and guards no obstacle to the power of God; the enemies' imprisonment overruled and undone by a higher power. The witnesses note Chrysostom's account of the purposes served: the deliverance was for the encouragement of the disciples (showing that God watched over them and could deliver) and for the instruction of the others (the rulers, who would learn that they fought against God); the miracle teaching both the church and its enemies. The expositors observe the manner of the deliverance and its purpose: the apostles were not delivered to flee and hide, but to continue their testimony (the next verse); the deliverance not for their ease but for their work — God freeing them to preach again. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ministry of angels in delivering God's servants, the power of God over the enemies' devices, and the deliverance that frees for further service. The verse thus records the angelic deliverance — the angel opening the prison by night — teaching the ministry of angels to God's people and the power of God that opened the prison men had shut.
20:
"Go, stand and speak in the temple to the people all the words of this life." The witnesses note the angel's commission to bold, public preaching. Gill draws out the charge: they were delivered "not in order to go and secure themselves from danger; but to go about the work they were called to, to preach the Gospel in the temple" — the public place — and there to "stand, and continue with courage and intrepidity, not fearing the reproach and wrath of men." Lightfoot notes "the words of this life" — the gospel of eternal life. The witnesses note the angel commanding the apostles to preach boldly and publicly in the temple "all the words of this life." The witnesses note the commission to bold preaching. The witnesses note the words of this life.

The witnesses note the angel's charge: to stand and preach boldly in the temple all the words of this life.

So the witnesses make the verse the angel's commission to bold, public preaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that the apostles were delivered not for their safety but for their service, to preach boldly the gospel of life. The witnesses note the charge: "Go, stand and speak in the temple to the people all the words of this life" — the angel, having freed them, commands them not to flee to safety but to return to the very temple, the public place, and there to preach openly to the people. The expositors observe Gill's point on the purpose of the deliverance: the apostles were not delivered to secure themselves from danger, but to go about their appointed work — to preach the gospel in the temple; the deliverance for service, not for self-preservation; God freeing them to do their duty, not to escape it. The witnesses press the boldness commanded: "stand and speak" — they were to stand firm, with courage and intrepidity (Gill), not fearing the reproach and wrath of men; to preach openly in the very place and to the very people from whom danger came; the boldness of those who have God on their side. The expositors draw the lesson on the courage of the gospel ministry: the apostles, just delivered from prison, are sent straight back to the danger, to preach boldly; the deliverance itself the encouragement to courage — God who delivered would keep them; the servant of Christ to fear God, not men, and to preach boldly despite opposition. The witnesses note the substance of the message: "all the words of this life" — the whole gospel, the words of eternal life (Lightfoot); the apostles to preach the full message of the life that is in Christ, holding back nothing; "the words of this life" the gospel that gives and offers life. The expositors observe the fullness commanded: "all the words" — not a part, but the whole counsel of God; the apostles to declare the entire gospel of life, the complete message of salvation in Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks that deliverance is for service, the courage commanded in the face of danger, and the fullness of the gospel of life to be preached. The verse thus records the angel's commission — stand and speak all the words of this life — teaching that the apostles were freed for bold, public, full preaching of the gospel of life.
21:
"And when they heard that, they entered into the temple early in the morning, and taught. But the high priest came, and they that were with him, and called the council together, and all the senate of the children of Israel, and sent to the prison to have them brought." The witnesses note the apostles' prompt obedience and the council's gathering. Gill notes they obeyed "the word of the angel," entering the temple at daybreak to teach. Barnes notes the council (the Sanhedrin) and "all the senate" (the eldership) gathered to try the apostles, sending to the prison to fetch them. The witnesses note the apostles obediently teaching at dawn, while the council assembled and sent for the prisoners they did not know were gone. The witnesses note the obedience and the council's gathering. The witnesses note the prompt obedience.

The witnesses note the apostles obeying at dawn — teaching in the temple while the council assembled to try them.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' obedience and the council's gathering. The lesson the expositors draw is the prompt, courageous obedience of the apostles and the unwitting folly of the council. The witnesses note the obedience: "when they heard that, they entered into the temple early in the morning, and taught" — the apostles, hearing the angel's charge, obeyed at once, entering the temple at daybreak to teach; their obedience prompt, courageous, and exact — going straight to the appointed place and work without delay or fear. The expositors observe the courage of this obedience: the apostles, just delivered from prison, returned at once to the public temple to preach — not hiding, not delaying, but openly resuming the very work for which they had been imprisoned; the boldness of those who feared God more than men, and trusted the God who had delivered them. The witnesses press the example: the apostles' obedience was immediate ("early in the morning"), exact (the temple, as commanded), and fearless (in the face of the danger from which they had just been freed); a pattern of ready obedience to God's word. The expositors note the council's gathering: "the high priest came... and called the council together, and all the senate of the children of Israel" — the Sanhedrin and the whole eldership (Barnes notes) assembled in full to try the apostles, sending to the prison to have them brought; the formal, official proceeding against them. The witnesses observe the dramatic irony: the council solemnly gathered and sent for the prisoners — not knowing they had been delivered and were at that moment teaching in the temple; the enemies' careful preparations made vain by the angel's deliverance, their solemn assembly about to be confounded. The expositors draw the lesson on the folly of opposing God: the council's elaborate proceedings, their full assembly, their sending for the prisoners, all rendered foolish by the deliverance they knew nothing of; the enemies of God laboring in vain, their plans overthrown before they began. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the prompt, courageous obedience of the apostles and the unwitting folly of those who oppose God. The verse thus records the apostles' obedience and the council's gathering — teaching at dawn while the council assembled and sent to the empty prison — teaching the ready obedience of the apostles and the confounding of the enemies' plans.
22:
"But when the officers came, and found them not in the prison, they returned, and told," The witnesses note the officers' discovery of the empty prison. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "the officers" (hypēretai, under-rowers) were "the servants who executed the orders of the Sanhedrin," and pictures the "perfect passive" — the prison "shut tight" with "the sentinels at the prison doors." The witnesses note the officers, sent to fetch the apostles, finding the prison empty and returning to report — the deliverance now coming to light. The witnesses note the empty prison discovered. The witnesses note the officers' report.

The witnesses note the officers finding the prison empty — the angelic deliverance coming to light.

So the witnesses make the verse the discovery of the empty prison. The lesson the expositors draw is the manifest evidence of the miraculous deliverance, which the officers could only report in bewilderment. The witnesses note the officers: "when the officers came" — the servants of the Sanhedrin (Robertson notes the under-rowers who executed its orders), sent to bring the apostles from prison to the council. The expositors observe their discovery: "found them not in the prison" — they came to the prison expecting to find the apostles securely held, but found it empty; the prisoners gone, though (the next verse) the prison was shut tight and the guards at their posts. The witnesses press the bewilderment this caused: the officers, finding the prison empty yet secure, could make nothing of it; they could only return and report what they had found — the inexplicable absence of the prisoners. The expositors draw the lesson on the manifest evidence of the deliverance: the empty prison, shut and guarded, was proof that the apostles had been delivered by no human means; the officers' report the first witness to the miracle, their bewilderment confirming that no natural explanation would serve. The witnesses observe the providence in the manner of the deliverance: God delivered the apostles in such a way as to leave the evidence plain — the prison shut, the guards posted, yet the prisoners gone; the miracle undeniable, the deliverance manifestly the work of God. The expositors note the confounding of the enemies: the officers' report would throw the council into perplexity (v.24); the careful security of the prison only heightening the wonder, the enemies confronted with a miracle they could not deny. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the manifest evidence of God's deliverance, the bewilderment of the enemies, and the providence that left the miracle undeniable. The verse thus records the discovery of the empty prison — the officers finding them gone and reporting — teaching the manifest evidence of the miraculous deliverance and the confounding of the enemies.
23:
"Saying, The prison truly found we shut with all safety, and the keepers standing without before the doors: but when we had opened, we found no man within." The witnesses note the officers' report establishing the miracle. Barnes presses the evidence: "it had not been broken open; and there was therefore clear proof that they had been delivered by the interposition of God. Nor could they have been released by the guard, for they were keeping watch... and the officers had the only means of entering." Clarke notes all was as they had left it — the doors bolted, the guards at their post — increasing their astonishment. The witnesses note the prison found shut and guarded, yet empty — the miracle established. The witnesses note the established miracle. The witnesses note the guarded but empty prison.

The witnesses note the prison shut and guarded yet empty — the miraculous deliverance established beyond doubt.

So the witnesses make the verse the report establishing the miracle. The lesson the expositors draw is the undeniable evidence of the divine deliverance, established by every circumstance. The witnesses note the officers' report: "The prison truly found we shut with all safety, and the keepers standing without before the doors: but when we had opened, we found no man within" — the prison was secure, locked and bolted, the guards at their posts; yet, when opened, it was empty. The expositors press the evidence Barnes marshals: the prison had not been broken open — so the apostles did not escape by force; the guards were at their posts, keeping watch — so they were not released by the keepers; and the officers had the only means of entering — so none had let the prisoners out; every natural explanation excluded, the deliverance manifestly the interposition of God. The witnesses observe Clarke's note on the astonishment: all was as the officers had left it — the doors bolted, the guards posted, everything in order — yet the prisoners gone; the perfect security only increasing the wonder, the miracle the more undeniable for the care that had failed to hold them. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty of the miracle: the deliverance was established by every circumstance — the locked doors, the posted guards, the empty cell; no fraud, no escape, no collusion possible; the hand of God plain to all. The witnesses note the futility of the enemies' precautions: all their care to secure the apostles — the strong prison, the locked doors, the watchful guards — availed nothing against the power of God; the enemies' security mocked, their precautions overthrown by a higher power. The expositors observe the confounding of the council: this report, establishing a miracle they could not deny, would throw the council into perplexity (v.24); the enemies confronted with the manifest power of God, unable to explain or to resist it. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the undeniable evidence of God's deliverance, the futility of the enemies' precautions, and the manifest hand of God that no human security could withstand. The verse thus records the report establishing the miracle — the prison shut and guarded yet empty — teaching the undeniable evidence of the divine deliverance and the overthrow of the enemies' precautions.
24:
"Now when the high priest and the captain of the temple and the chief priests heard these things, they doubted of them whereunto this would grow." The witnesses note the perplexity of the rulers. Barnes explains "doubted" denotes "that state of anxiety which arises when a man has lost his way, or when he does not know what to do to escape from a difficulty"; they were "in perplexity," not knowing "what would be the result or end of these events" — for their authority was disregarded, God had opposed them by a miracle, and the apostles' doctrine spread. The witnesses note the rulers, hearing the report, thrown into perplexity and anxiety, not knowing where this would lead. The witnesses note the rulers' perplexity. The witnesses note the rulers at a loss.

The witnesses note the rulers' perplexity — at a loss, anxious where the apostles' growing power would end.

So the witnesses make the verse the perplexity of the rulers. The lesson the expositors draw is the bewilderment and anxiety of those who oppose God, confounded by his manifest power. The witnesses note the rulers' state: "they doubted of them whereunto this would grow" — Barnes explains "doubted" denotes the anxiety of a man who has lost his way, who does not know how to escape his difficulty; the rulers were in perplexity, at a loss, not knowing what to do or where these events would lead. The expositors observe the grounds of their perplexity: their authority was disregarded (the apostles preached on despite their threats and imprisonment); God had opposed them by a manifest miracle (the deliverance from the locked prison); and the apostles' doctrine spread and prevailed among the people; the rulers confronted with a power they could not master and a movement they could not stop. The witnesses press the anxiety implied: "whereunto this would grow" — they feared the growth of the movement, anxious where it would end; seeing the apostles delivered, undeterred, preaching on, and the people drawn to them, they dreaded the spread of the gospel and the loss of their own authority. The expositors draw the lesson on the helplessness of those who fight against God: the rulers, with all their power, were at a loss — their imprisonment undone, their threats ignored, their authority flouted; the opposers of God reduced to bewilderment, unable to explain or resist his work; "he that sitteth in the heavens shall laugh" (Psalm 2:4) at the vain plotting of the enemies. The witnesses observe the contrast with the apostles' confidence: while the rulers were perplexed and anxious, the apostles were bold and assured, preaching with confidence; the enemies of God in confusion, the servants of God in peace — the difference between those who fight against God and those who serve him. The expositors note the providence in the rulers' perplexity: their bewilderment a token of God's overruling, the enemies confounded that his work might go forward; the perplexity of the opposers the foil to the advance of the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the perplexity and anxiety of those who oppose God, the helplessness of fighting against his work, and the contrast with the confidence of his servants. The verse thus records the rulers' perplexity — at a loss where this would grow — teaching the bewilderment and anxiety of those who oppose God, confounded by his manifest power.
25:
"Then came one and told them, saying, Behold, the men whom ye put in prison are standing in the temple, and teaching the people." The witnesses note the startling news brought to the perplexed council. Clarke notes that "while they were in the perplexity mentioned above, a messenger surprised them with the information that the very men whom they had imprisoned the preceding night were standing in the temple and teaching the people!" Gill supposes the informant an enemy seeking the rulers' favor. The witnesses note the messenger's report that the apostles, far from fleeing, were boldly teaching in the temple — the news deepening the council's confusion. The witnesses note the startling news. The witnesses note the apostles boldly teaching.

The witnesses note the news: the imprisoned apostles boldly teaching in the temple — deepening the council's dismay.

So the witnesses make the verse the startling news brought to the council. The lesson the expositors draw is the boldness of the apostles and the deepening confusion of their enemies. The witnesses note the report: "Behold, the men whom ye put in prison are standing in the temple, and teaching the people" — a messenger brings the council the astonishing news that the very men they had imprisoned were now openly teaching in the temple; Clarke marks the surprise — while the rulers were in their perplexity, this news surprised them further. The expositors observe the boldness of the apostles it reveals: not only had the apostles been delivered, but they had returned to the public temple to teach — not fleeing, not hiding, but boldly resuming their work in the very place of danger; their courage astonishing, their confidence in God plain. The witnesses press the deepening of the council's confusion: the rulers, already perplexed by the empty prison, now learn that the prisoners are publicly preaching — the news compounding their bewilderment, the apostles' boldness mocking their authority; the enemies confounded the more. The expositors draw the lesson on the contrast of boldness and dismay: the apostles bold and undeterred, teaching openly; the rulers anxious and confounded, not knowing what to do; the servants of God advancing, the enemies of God in confusion — the boldness of faith against the perplexity of opposition.
