The witnesses note Gill's supposition about the informant: likely an enemy of the apostles, seeking the favor of the rulers by bringing the report; the news carried by one hostile to the gospel, yet serving, in God's providence, to display the apostles' boldness. The expositors observe the providence in the news: the report, meant to aid the rulers, only displayed the futility of their opposition — the prisoners they had jailed now teaching in the temple, the enemies' power shown vain; the news advancing the testimony to God's deliverance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the boldness of the apostles, the deepening dismay of the enemies, and the providence that turned even the hostile report to the display of God's power. The verse thus records the startling news — the imprisoned apostles teaching in the temple — teaching the boldness of the apostles and the deepening confusion of their enemies.
26:
"Then went the captain with the officers, and brought them without violence: for they feared the people, lest they should have been stoned." The witnesses note the careful, fearful re-arrest. Matthew Henry notes the second seizing of the apostles, observing that their first deliverance "was designed to humble the pride, and check the fury, of their persecutors." Gill identifies the captain of the temple bringing them without force. The witnesses note the captain and officers bringing the apostles gently, without violence, for fear of the people who esteemed them — the enemies restrained by fear. The witnesses note the fearful re-arrest. The witnesses note the enemies restrained by fear.

The witnesses note the apostles brought without violence — the enemies restrained by fear of the people.

So the witnesses make the verse the careful, fearful re-arrest. The lesson the expositors draw is the restraint of the enemies by fear of the people, and the providence that protected the apostles. The witnesses note the manner of the arrest: "the captain with the officers... brought them without violence" — the apostles were taken gently, without force or rough handling; the enemies, for all their indignation, dared not use violence. The expositors observe the reason: "for they feared the people, lest they should have been stoned" — the rulers feared the people, who esteemed the apostles (v.13) and were moved by the miracles; afraid that violence against the apostles would provoke the people to stone the officers; the enemies restrained by fear. The witnesses press the providence in this fear: God used the people's esteem to restrain the enemies' violence — the apostles protected, not by their own power, but by the fear that God put into their enemies; the people's regard a shield, the enemies' fear a leash; the providence guarding the apostles through the very fear of those who hated them. The expositors draw the lesson on God's protection of his servants: the enemies, who would gladly have used violence, were held back by fear; the apostles preserved from rough handling by the providence that turned the people's esteem and the rulers' fear to their protection; God restraining the wrath of men (Psalm 76:10). The witnesses note Matthew Henry's observation on the deliverance: the apostles' first deliverance from prison was designed to humble the pride and check the fury of the persecutors — showing them that they fought against a power they could not master; and now the fear that restrained their violence further checked their fury. The expositors observe the contrast: the rulers, full of indignation and jealousy (v.17), yet forced to act gently for fear of the people; their hatred restrained, their violence checked, by the providence of God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the restraint of the enemies by fear, the providence that protects God's servants, and the God who curbs the wrath of men. The verse thus records the careful re-arrest — brought without violence for fear of the people — teaching the restraint of the enemies by fear and the providence that protected the apostles.
27:
"And when they had brought them, they set them before the council: and the high priest asked them," The witnesses note the apostles set before the Sanhedrin for the second time. Gill notes they were brought from the temple to the place where the Sanhedrin sat. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "they set them" (transitive) before the council. The witnesses note the apostles standing again before the council, the high priest about to question them — the formal proceeding resuming after the deliverance. The witnesses note the apostles before the council. The witnesses note the second arraignment.

The witnesses note the apostles set again before the council — the formal proceeding resuming after the deliverance.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles arraigned before the council. The lesson the expositors draw is the renewed confrontation between the apostles and the council, the stage set for the bold testimony to come. The witnesses note the bringing: "when they had brought them, they set them before the council" — the apostles, brought from the temple, were set before the Sanhedrin (Gill notes, in its place of session); Robertson marks the transitive "they set them," the apostles placed formally before the assembled court. The expositors observe the renewed confrontation: the apostles, delivered from prison and arrested again, now stand before the very council that had imprisoned them; the confrontation resuming, the enemies and the servants of God face to face once more. The witnesses press the boldness of the apostles' position: they stand before the full Sanhedrin, the highest court of the nation, having defied its threats and continued their preaching; the prisoners delivered by an angel, now arraigned again, yet undaunted, ready to testify. The expositors draw the lesson on the courage of the witnesses: the apostles, far from cowed by the council's power or their own imprisonment, stand bold before the court, prepared to confess Christ again; the boldness of those who fear God more than men, assured of his presence and deliverance. The witnesses note the high priest's question (begun here, stated in v.28): the high priest, presiding, prepares to charge the apostles with disobeying the council's command; the formal accusation about to be made. The expositors observe the stage set for the testimony: the council's question would draw from the apostles their great confession — "we ought to obey God rather than men" (v.29); the arraignment the occasion for the bold witness; the enemies' proceeding turned to the advancement of the testimony. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the renewed confrontation, the boldness of the apostles before the court, and the providence that made the arraignment the occasion for testimony. The verse thus records the apostles set before the council — brought and arraigned, the high priest questioning — teaching the renewed confrontation and the stage set for the bold testimony to come.
28:
"Saying, Did not we straitly command you that ye should not teach in this name? and, behold, ye have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine, and intend to bring this man's blood upon us." The witnesses note the council's charge and its unwitting tribute. Barnes observes the charge "ye have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine" was, "though not so designed, an honourable tribute to the zeal and fidelity of the apostles." He notes "to bring this man's blood upon us" means to hold them guilty of Christ's death — which they would not name, saying "this man." The witnesses note the high priest charging the apostles with disobeying the command not to teach in Christ's name, and with charging them with his blood. The witnesses note the council's charge. The witnesses note the unwitting tribute.

The witnesses note the council's charge — unwittingly praising the apostles' success and shrinking from Christ's blood.

So the witnesses make the verse the council's charge. The lesson the expositors draw is the unwitting tribute to the apostles' faithfulness, and the rulers' guilty fear of Christ's blood. The witnesses note the first part of the charge: "Did not we straitly command you that ye should not teach in this name?" — the high priest charges the apostles with disobeying the council's strict command (Acts 4:18) not to teach in the name of Jesus; the rulers indignant that their authority had been flouted. The expositors observe the unwitting tribute Barnes marks in the second part: "ye have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine" — the charge, though meant as accusation, was an honorable testimony to the apostles' zeal and success; they had filled the whole city with the gospel, their doctrine spreading everywhere; the enemies themselves witnessing to the triumph of the gospel. The witnesses press the lesson on the success of faithful witness: the apostles' "crime," in the eyes of their enemies, was that they had filled Jerusalem with the gospel — a charge any faithful witness might be glad to bear; "if any man suffer as a Christian, let him not be ashamed" (1 Peter 4:16); the enemies' accusation the believer's commendation. The expositors note the third part: "intend to bring this man's blood upon us" — the rulers fear that the apostles are charging them with the guilt of Christ's death, holding them responsible for his murder; their guilty conscience betrayed by the fear. The witnesses observe the telling phrase "this man" — the rulers would not even name Jesus, but spoke of him with contempt as "this man"; their refusal to name him a token of their hatred and their guilt. The expositors draw the lesson on the rulers' guilty fear: they who had cried "his blood be on us, and on our children" (Matthew 27:25) now shrink from the charge of his blood; their conscience accusing them, their guilt pressing on them; the murderers of Christ afraid to hear of his blood. The witnesses note the irony: the apostles preached Christ for the people's salvation, not merely to charge the rulers; yet the rulers, guilty, heard only accusation; their own conscience turning the gospel into a charge against them. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unwitting tribute to faithful witness, the success that fills a city with the gospel, and the guilty fear of those who reject Christ. The verse thus records the council's charge — disobeying the command, filling Jerusalem, Christ's blood — teaching the unwitting tribute to the apostles' faithfulness and the rulers' guilty fear of Christ's blood.
29:
"Then Peter and the other apostles answered and said, We ought to obey God rather than men." The witnesses note the apostles' great principle of obedience to God. Ellicott notes "the whole company of the Twelve were now the objects of attack, and they all accept Peter as their spokesman." Clarke notes this was "the same answer they gave before (Acts 4:19), founded on the same reason... We have received our commission from God; we dare not lay it down at the desire or command of men." The witnesses note Peter and the apostles asserting the great principle: when the commands of men conflict with the command of God, God must be obeyed. The witnesses note the principle of obeying God. The witnesses note the apostles' bold answer.

The witnesses note the apostles' great principle: we must obey God rather than men.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' principle of obedience to God. The lesson the expositors draw is the supremacy of God's command over man's, when the two conflict. The witnesses note the bold answer: "Peter and the other apostles answered and said" — Ellicott notes the whole Twelve were now under attack, and all accept Peter as their spokesman; the apostles together making their stand. The expositors observe the great principle: "We ought to obey God rather than men" — Clarke notes this was the same answer they had given before (Acts 4:19), on the same ground: they had received their commission from God, and dared not lay it down at the command of men; God's authority supreme over man's. The witnesses press the meaning of the principle: when the command of man conflicts with the command of God, God must be obeyed; the apostles, commanded by God to preach (v.20) and by the council not to preach, must obey God; no human authority can bind the conscience against God's clear command. The expositors draw the lesson on the limits of human authority: civil and religious authorities are to be obeyed in their proper sphere (Romans 13:1); but when they command what God forbids, or forbid what God commands, the believer must obey God; the conscience bound to God above all human power. The witnesses note the boldness and the reverence of the answer: the apostles do not defy authority wantonly, nor rebel for its own sake, but humbly assert that they must obey God; the principle stated with reverence, not arrogance — a matter of conscience, not of pride. The expositors observe the foundation of Christian liberty of conscience: this principle is the charter of conscience against tyranny — that God must be obeyed before men; the ground on which martyrs have stood, refusing to deny Christ at the command of men; the supremacy of God's authority over every human power. The witnesses draw the application: the believer holds the great principle — to obey God rather than men when the two conflict; to submit to authority in its sphere, but to obey God above all; the conscience bound to God alone. The verse thus records the apostles' principle — we ought to obey God rather than men — teaching the supremacy of God's command over man's and the charter of conscience against tyranny.
30:
"The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom ye slew and hanged on a tree." The witnesses note the apostles' testimony to the God of the fathers and the charge of Christ's death. Gill takes "raised up Jesus" here not of the resurrection but of God's "calling him to the work and office of a Saviour... in consequence of the ancient council and covenant of grace"; the apostles attributing this to "the God of our fathers" to show they preached no strange God. Barnes takes "raised up" of the resurrection. The witnesses note the apostles testifying that the God of the fathers raised up Jesus, whom the rulers slew, hanging him on a tree. The witnesses note the testimony to Christ. The witnesses note the charge of his death.

The witnesses note the apostles' testimony: the God of the fathers raised up Jesus, whom the rulers slew on the tree.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' testimony to Christ and the charge of his death. The lesson the expositors draw is that the God of the fathers raised up Jesus, in contrast to the rulers who slew him. The witnesses note the grounding in the God of the fathers: "The God of our fathers raised up Jesus" — the apostles attribute their message to the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Gill notes), to show they preached no strange God, but the God of Israel, who had now raised up Jesus; the gospel the fulfilment of the faith of the fathers. The expositors weigh the sense of "raised up Jesus." Gill takes it not of the resurrection but of God's raising him up to the office of Saviour — calling, sending, and exhibiting him in the flesh, in consequence of the ancient covenant of grace; the same sense as the prophet "raised up" (Acts 3:22). Barnes takes it of the resurrection — God raised him from the dead. The witnesses observe that both senses are true and may be embraced: God raised up Jesus as the Saviour (sending him), and raised him from the dead (vindicating him); the apostles testifying to the God who both sent and raised Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the contrast Peter draws: "the God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom ye slew" — God exalted him; the rulers killed him; the divine honor set against the human crime; the same Jesus raised up by God and slain by men. The witnesses note the charge "whom ye slew and hanged on a tree" — Peter charges the rulers plainly with Christ's death (they had instigated his crucifixion); and "hanged on a tree" recalls the curse of the law (Deuteronomy 21:23, "cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree"), pointing to Christ bearing the curse for us (Galatians 3:13). The expositors observe the boldness of the testimony: the apostles, before the very council that condemned Christ, charge them with his death and proclaim his exaltation; the boldest confession, made to the murderers' faces. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God of the fathers who raised up Jesus, the contrast with the rulers who slew him, and the curse he bore on the tree for us. The verse thus records the apostles' testimony — the God of the fathers raised up Jesus, whom ye slew on the tree — teaching the divine exaltation of Christ against the human crime, and the curse he bore for us.
31:
"Him hath God exalted with his right hand to be a Prince and a Saviour, for to give repentance to Israel, and forgiveness of sins." The witnesses note the exaltation of Christ as Prince and Saviour and the gifts he bestows. Gill explains "exalted with his right hand" as "by his power" — raised from the dead and exalted to the right hand of God, crowned with glory, having all power in heaven and earth. Barnes notes "a Prince" (archēgon, as in Acts 3:15) denotes dominion and royal authority. The witnesses note Christ exalted to be a Prince (with sovereign authority) and a Saviour (with saving power), who gives repentance and forgiveness — even to Israel who slew him. The witnesses note Christ exalted as Prince and Saviour. The witnesses note the gifts of repentance and forgiveness.

The witnesses note Christ exalted as Prince and Saviour — giving repentance and forgiveness, even to Israel who slew him.

So the witnesses make the verse the exaltation of Christ and the gifts he bestows. The lesson the expositors draw is that the slain Jesus is exalted to be Prince and Saviour, giving repentance and forgiveness even to those who killed him. The witnesses note the exaltation: "Him hath God exalted with his right hand" — Gill explains "with his right hand" as by God's power; the Jesus the rulers slew, God raised from the dead and exalted to the highest place, the right hand of God, crowned with glory and honor, given all power in heaven and earth. The expositors observe the two offices: "to be a Prince and a Saviour" — Christ exalted as a Prince (Barnes notes archēgon, leader, sovereign, the same word as "the Prince of life," Acts 3:15), with royal dominion and authority; and as a Saviour, with the power to save; the exalted Christ both Ruler and Redeemer. The witnesses press the gifts he bestows: "for to give repentance to Israel, and forgiveness of sins" — the exalted Christ gives repentance and forgiveness; both the turning of the heart from sin (repentance) and the pardon of sin (forgiveness) are his gifts, bestowed from his throne. The expositors draw the lesson on repentance as a gift: repentance is not merely man's act, but Christ's gift — the exalted Saviour grants it, turning the heart to God; "him hath God exalted... to give repentance" (so 2 Timothy 2:25); the grace of repentance flowing from the throne of Christ. The witnesses note the wonder of the recipients: "to Israel" — the repentance and forgiveness are offered even to Israel, the very nation that slew Christ; the exalted Saviour giving the grace of repentance and pardon to his own murderers; the riches of grace, that the slain Prince should bestow forgiveness on those who killed him. The expositors observe the connection of the offices and the gifts: Christ is exalted as Prince (to rule) and Saviour (to save), and as such gives repentance (the Prince subduing the heart) and forgiveness (the Saviour pardoning the sin); his royal and saving offices joined in the bestowal of grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the exaltation of the slain Christ as Prince and Saviour, the gifts of repentance and forgiveness as his to bestow, and the grace that offers them even to his murderers. The verse thus records the exaltation of Christ and his gifts — Prince and Saviour, giving repentance and forgiveness — teaching the exaltation of the slain Jesus and the grace he bestows even on those who killed him.
32:
"And we are his witnesses of these things; and so is also the Holy Ghost, whom God hath given to them that obey him." The witnesses note the double witness to Christ — the apostles and the Holy Ghost. Gill lists what the apostles witnessed: the incarnation, crucifixion, resurrection, exaltation, and offices of Christ, and the influences of his grace. Barnes notes they were appointed witnesses (Acts 1:8), and that the Holy Ghost also witnessed by his descent and the miracles. The witnesses note the apostles testifying as witnesses, and the Holy Ghost (given to the obedient) joining his witness to theirs — the double testimony confirming the gospel. The witnesses note the double witness. The witnesses note the Spirit given to the obedient.

The witnesses note the double witness — the apostles and the Holy Ghost — confirming the gospel of the exalted Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the double witness to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the joint testimony of the apostles and the Holy Ghost, confirming the truth of the gospel. The witnesses note the apostolic witness: "we are his witnesses of these things" — the apostles testify to the things they have declared; Gill lists the scope — the incarnation, crucifixion, resurrection, exaltation, and saving offices of Christ, and the influences of his grace; the apostles appointed witnesses (Acts 1:8), testifying from personal knowledge. The expositors observe the second witness: "and so is also the Holy Ghost" — the Holy Ghost joins his testimony to the apostles'; the Spirit witnessing by his descent at Pentecost, by the miracles wrought, by the conversion of souls, and by his inward testimony in believers; the divine witness confirming the human. The witnesses press the weight of the double testimony: the gospel is attested by two witnesses — the apostles (who saw and testify) and the Holy Ghost (who confirms by his power and presence); "in the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established" (Deuteronomy 19:15); the testimony of the apostles confirmed and sealed by the testimony of the Spirit. The expositors draw the lesson on the confirmation of the gospel: the gospel rests not on the apostles' word alone, but on the joint witness of the apostles and the Spirit; the human testimony backed by the divine; the Spirit's power and presence the seal of the apostles' message. The witnesses note the condition: "whom God hath given to them that obey him" — the Holy Ghost is given to the obedient; not to the rebellious, but to those who obey God; the Spirit's gift and witness joined to obedience. The expositors weigh the sense of "to them that obey him": the Spirit is given to those who obey the gospel, who believe and submit to Christ; obedience (the obedience of faith) the way to receive the Spirit; the gift of the Spirit attending the obedience of faith, not the resistance of unbelief. The witnesses observe the implicit rebuke to the rulers: the Spirit is given to the obedient — but the rulers, disobedient and resisting, lacked the Spirit and his witness; their rejection cutting them off from the gift. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the double witness of the apostles and the Spirit, marks the Spirit given to the obedient, and seeks the Spirit's witness through the obedience of faith. The verse thus records the double witness — the apostles and the Holy Ghost, given to the obedient — teaching the joint testimony confirming the gospel and the Spirit given to those who obey.
33:
"When they heard that, they were cut to the heart, and took counsel to slay them." The witnesses note the rulers' rage and murderous intent. Barnes explains "cut to the heart" — the word "properly denotes to cut with a saw; as applied to the mind, to be agitated with rage and indignation, as if wrath should seize upon the mind as a saw does upon wood, and tear it violently" (cf. Acts 7:54). The witnesses note the rulers, hearing the apostles' bold testimony — that they had slain Christ, whom God exalted — being filled with rage and taking counsel to kill the apostles. The witnesses note the rulers' murderous rage. The witnesses note the cut to the heart.

The witnesses note the rulers cut to the heart with rage — taking counsel to slay the apostles.

So the witnesses make the verse the rulers' rage and murderous intent. The lesson the expositors draw is the fury of those convicted yet unrepentant, who answer the truth with rage and the will to kill. The witnesses note the effect of the testimony: "when they heard that, they were cut to the heart" — Barnes explains the word denotes to be sawn through, the mind agitated with rage and indignation, as a saw tears wood; the rulers, hearing the apostles' bold charge (that they had slain Christ, whom God exalted) and the claim of the Spirit's witness, were torn with fury. The expositors observe the contrast with the right kind of being "pricked": at Pentecost, the hearers were "pricked in their heart" (Acts 2:37) unto repentance; here the rulers are "cut to the heart" unto rage — the same truth producing, in the penitent, contrition, and in the hardened, fury; the difference in the heart, not the message. The witnesses press the murderous intent: "and took counsel to slay them" — the rulers, convicted but unrepentant, resolved to kill the apostles; their rage issuing in the will to murder, as it had against Christ; the truth that should have humbled them only hardening and enraging them. The expositors draw the lesson on the effect of truth on the hardened heart: the same word that softens the penitent hardens the impenitent; conviction without repentance turns to rage; the rulers, unwilling to repent, would silence the truth by killing its messengers — the recourse of those who cannot answer but will not yield. The witnesses observe the desperate wickedness: the rulers, confronted with the manifest power of God (the miracles, the deliverance), and convicted of the murder of Christ, yet resolve to murder again — adding the intended blood of the apostles to the blood of their Lord; the depth of hardened sin, that fights against God to the end. The expositors note the providence that would restrain them (the next verses, Gamaliel's counsel): God would check their murderous intent, sparing the apostles for further service; the rage permitted, but bounded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fury of the convicted but unrepentant, the difference between being pricked unto repentance and cut unto rage, and the desperate wickedness that answers truth with murder. The verse thus records the rulers' rage and murderous intent — cut to the heart, taking counsel to slay them — teaching the fury of the hardened heart that answers conviction with rage and the will to kill.
34:
"Then stood there up one in the council, a Pharisee, named Gamaliel, a doctor of the law, had in reputation among all the people, and commanded to put the apostles forth a little space;" The witnesses note the rising of Gamaliel and his standing. Chrysostom identifies him as "Paul's teacher" (Acts 22:3), and wonders that one "so right-minded in his judgment, and withal learned in the law, did not yet believe"; yet thinks "it cannot be that he should have continued in unbelief to the end." Chrysostom notes Gamaliel's prudent management — addressing the council "as if he and they were of the same opinion," not with vehemence. The witnesses note Gamaliel, the respected Pharisee and teacher of the law, rising and ordering the apostles put out while he counseled the council. The witnesses note Gamaliel's intervention. The witnesses note the respected teacher.

The witnesses note Gamaliel rising — the respected Pharisee and Paul's teacher — to counsel the enraged council.

So the witnesses make the verse the rising of Gamaliel. The lesson the expositors draw is the providential intervention of a respected and prudent counselor to restrain the council's murderous rage. The witnesses note the man: "a Pharisee, named Gamaliel, a doctor of the law, had in reputation among all the people" — a Pharisee (more open to the resurrection than the Sadducees), a renowned teacher of the law, highly esteemed by all the people; a man whose word would carry weight in the council. The expositors observe Chrysostom's identification: Gamaliel was Paul's teacher (Acts 22:3, "brought up at the feet of Gamaliel"); and Chrysostom wonders that one so right-minded and learned did not yet believe, yet thinks he could not have continued in unbelief to the end — a charitable hope, though Scripture does not record his conversion. The witnesses note the providence in his rising: at the moment the council was resolved to slay the apostles (v.33), this respected and moderate counselor stood up to restrain them; God raising up, even within the hostile council, a voice of caution to spare the apostles; the providence that checks the wrath of the enemies. The expositors draw out Chrysostom's note on Gamaliel's prudence: he commanded the apostles put out a little space, then addressed the council with skill — speaking as if he and they were of the same opinion (so as not to be suspected of taking the apostles' part), not with vehemence, but calmly framing his counsel to cool their passion. The witnesses press the wisdom of his management: Gamaliel, knowing the council's rage, did not directly oppose them, but won their ear by appearing to share their concern, then gently turned them from their murderous purpose; the prudence of a wise counselor restraining a passionate assembly. The expositors observe the limits of Gamaliel's stand: he counseled caution and neutrality, not faith; he would have the council wait and see, not believe and obey; a prudent moderation, but not a confession of Christ; the difference between political caution and saving faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that raised up a respected counselor to restrain the rage, the prudence of Gamaliel's management, and the difference between cautious neutrality and saving faith. The verse thus records the rising of Gamaliel — the respected teacher counseling the council — teaching the providential intervention that restrained the murderous rage.
35:
"And said unto them, Ye men of Israel, take heed to yourselves what ye intend to do as touching these men." The witnesses note Gamaliel's opening caution. Chrysostom notes how Gamaliel "immediately at the very outset puts them in fear," speaking to men "intoxicated through passion" — "Do not go to work rashly." Clarke notes "what ye intend to do" means "what ye are about to do" — they had already intended to destroy them, and were now about to do it. The witnesses note Gamaliel warning the council to take heed and act cautiously concerning the apostles, checking their rash, murderous intent. The witnesses note Gamaliel's caution. The witnesses note the warning against rashness.

The witnesses note Gamaliel's caution — warning the council to take heed and not act rashly against the apostles.

So the witnesses make the verse Gamaliel's opening caution. The lesson the expositors draw is the wise warning against rash action, restraining the council's passion. The witnesses note the address: "Ye men of Israel" — Gamaliel addresses the council with the familiar, weighty address of a respected elder; and his counsel: "take heed to yourselves what ye intend to do as touching these men" — a warning to consider carefully, to act with caution, not rashly. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the skill of the opening: Gamaliel, at the very outset, puts the council in fear — warning them to take heed to themselves, as if their own welfare were at stake; speaking to men intoxicated with passion, he counsels deliberation, not haste; cooling their rage by appealing to their own interest. The witnesses note Clarke's clarification of "what ye intend to do": it means what they were about to do — they had already resolved to destroy the apostles (v.33), and were now on the point of doing it; Gamaliel checks them at the very edge of the murderous act. The expositors draw the lesson on the wisdom of caution: Gamaliel, seeing the council about to act rashly in passion, counsels them to take heed — to weigh the matter, to consider the consequences, not to rush into an irreversible deed; the wisdom of deliberation against the folly of passionate haste. The witnesses press the providence in this restraint: God used Gamaliel's caution to check the council's murderous intent, sparing the apostles; the wise word of a respected counselor turning the assembly from rash murder to deliberation; the providence that bounds the wrath of the enemies. The expositors observe the appeal to self-interest: Gamaliel warns them to "take heed to yourselves" — appealing to their own safety and reputation, lest they act in a way they would regret; a prudent appeal, calculated to cool their passion by self-concern. The witnesses note the limits of the counsel: Gamaliel counsels caution, not justice; deliberation, not the release of the innocent; his concern that the council not act rashly, not that it do right; political prudence, not the vindication of the apostles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the wise warning against rash action, the providence that restrained the rage, and the prudence (short of justice) of Gamaliel's caution. The verse thus records Gamaliel's caution — take heed what ye intend to do — teaching the wise warning against rash action that restrained the council's murderous passion.
36:
"For before these days rose up Theudas, boasting himself to be somebody; to whom a number of men, about four hundred, joined themselves: who was slain; and all, as many as obeyed him, were scattered, and brought to nought." The witnesses note Gamaliel's first example and a chronological difficulty. Gill notes Josephus speaks of a Theudas who claimed to be a prophet and drew many, slain by Cuspius Fadus; "this account agrees with this instance of Gamaliel, only differs in chronology," since Josephus's Theudas was later. The witnesses note Gamaliel citing Theudas, a pretender whose movement came to nothing when he was slain — the example reconciled with Josephus by supposing an earlier Theudas, or Josephus's imprecision. The witnesses note the example of Theudas. The witnesses note the failed pretender.

The witnesses note Gamaliel's first example — Theudas, whose movement collapsed at his death (with a noted chronological difficulty).

So the witnesses make the verse Gamaliel's first example, Theudas. The lesson the expositors draw is the principle that merely human movements collapse, and the honest handling of a chronological difficulty. The witnesses note the example: "before these days rose up Theudas, boasting himself to be somebody" — Gamaliel cites a pretender named Theudas, who claimed to be someone great (a prophet or leader), drew a following of about four hundred, but was slain, and his followers scattered and brought to nothing; a movement that, being merely human, perished with its leader. The expositors observe the principle Gamaliel illustrates: merely human movements, founded on the pretensions of a man, come to nothing when the man fails; the collapse of Theudas's movement at his death the proof that it was not of God; the argument Gamaliel will apply to the apostles (v.38). The witnesses note the chronological difficulty Gill raises: Josephus mentions a Theudas who claimed to be a prophet and was slain by Cuspius Fadus — but that Theudas arose later than Gamaliel's speech, so cannot be the one Gamaliel names; the difficulty being that Gamaliel's Theudas was "some time ago," before this speech. The expositors weigh the harmonization: most hold that Gamaliel refers to an earlier Theudas (the name being common, and such pretenders frequent in those turbulent times), distinct from Josephus's later one; or that Josephus, not always precise in chronology, is in error; in either case, no real contradiction, for there may well have been two Theudases, or Josephus may have misdated. The witnesses observe the honest handling of the difficulty: the witnesses do not hide the problem, but show the likely solution — an earlier Theudas, the name common, the times full of such risings; the credibility of Acts not impugned by a difficulty so readily met. The expositors note the broader point: such pretenders and their failed movements were common in that age, and Gamaliel's hearers would know them well; the example apt and familiar, whatever the precise identity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the principle that human movements collapse, the honest handling of the chronological difficulty, and the likely solution of an earlier Theudas. The verse thus records Gamaliel's first example — Theudas, whose movement came to nothing — teaching the principle that merely human movements perish, and the credibility of Acts amid a noted chronological difficulty.
37:
"After this man rose up Judas of Galilee in the days of the taxing, and drew away much people after him: he also perished; and all, even as many as obeyed him, were dispersed." The witnesses note Gamaliel's second example, Judas of Galilee. Barnes notes Josephus gives an account of this Judas (a Galilean, of Gamala), whose revolt took place under Cyrenius "in the days of the taxing" (the census); his movement also failing at his death. Lightfoot discusses the name (Judas the Gaulanite, called of Galilee). The witnesses note Gamaliel citing Judas of Galilee, who led a revolt at the time of the census but perished, his followers dispersed — a second example of a failed human movement. The witnesses note the example of Judas. The witnesses note the second failed rising.

The witnesses note Gamaliel's second example — Judas of Galilee, whose revolt also perished with its leader.

So the witnesses make the verse Gamaliel's second example, Judas of Galilee. The lesson the expositors draw is the repeated principle that human movements perish, confirmed by a second well-known instance. The witnesses note the example: "after this man rose up Judas of Galilee in the days of the taxing, and drew away much people after him" — Gamaliel cites a second pretender, Judas of Galilee, who led a revolt at the time of the census (Barnes notes, under Cyrenius/Quirinius; Josephus records him, a Galilean of Gamala); he drew much people, but "he also perished, and all, even as many as obeyed him, were dispersed." The expositors observe the historical familiarity of this example: Judas of Galilee's revolt was well-known (recorded by Josephus), a notable rising against the Roman taxation, which collapsed at his death; Gamaliel's hearers would know it well, the example apt and undisputed. The witnesses note Lightfoot's discussion of the name (Judas the Gaulanite, called "of Galilee" from the region); a minor point of identification, the man himself well-attested. The expositors press the repeated principle: as with Theudas, so with Judas — the movement, being merely human, perished with its leader; the followers dispersed, the rising come to nothing; the second example confirming the first, that human enterprises fail. The witnesses observe the cumulative force of the two examples: Gamaliel cites two recent, well-known instances of failed movements, both founded on a man's pretensions, both collapsing at the man's death; the pattern clear — what is of men comes to nothing. The expositors draw out Gamaliel's argument building to its conclusion (v.38-39): if the apostles' movement is like these, merely human, it will collapse of itself; the council need not trouble to destroy it. The witnesses note the difference the council could not yet see: the apostles' movement, unlike Theudas's and Judas's, was of God — and so, by Gamaliel's own principle, could not be overthrown; the very examples Gamaliel cites pointing, in the event, to the opposite conclusion, for the gospel did not fail. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the repeated principle that human movements perish, the historical examples, and the contrast with the gospel that, being of God, endured. The verse thus records Gamaliel's second example — Judas of Galilee, whose revolt perished — teaching the repeated principle that merely human movements fail, building to Gamaliel's counsel.
38:
"And now I say unto you, Refrain from these men, and let them alone: for if this counsel or this work be of men, it will come to nought:" The witnesses note Gamaliel's counsel and its first principle. Barnes explains the advice: "cease to oppose them, or to threaten them," for if the movement "had been originated for purposes of imposture, it will come to nought," as the examples showed; and notes carefully that "this is the mere advice of Gamaliel, who was not inspired, and that this opinion should not be adduced to guide us, except as an instance of great wisdom and prudence in a particular case." The witnesses note Gamaliel counseling the council to leave the apostles alone, on the principle that a merely human work will fail of itself. The witnesses note Gamaliel's counsel. The witnesses note the first principle.

The witnesses note Gamaliel's counsel: leave the apostles alone — a merely human work will come to nothing of itself.

So the witnesses make the verse Gamaliel's counsel and its first principle. The lesson the expositors draw is the prudent advice to let the apostles alone, grounded on the principle that human works fail — true in general, but offered as uninspired counsel. The witnesses note the advice: "Refrain from these men, and let them alone" — Gamaliel counsels the council to cease opposing and threatening the apostles, to leave them be; not to persecute, but to wait and see. The expositors observe the first ground: "for if this counsel or this work be of men, it will come to nought" — if the apostles' movement is merely human, founded on imposture or human pretension (like Theudas's and Judas's), it will collapse of itself, as those did; the council need not trouble to destroy it, for time and providence will end it. The witnesses press the principle: merely human works and movements, lacking God's support, fail and come to nothing; if the gospel were such, it would die out of itself; the council's persecution unnecessary, for a false movement perishes without help. The expositors draw out the wisdom of the counsel: Gamaliel's advice was prudent — to avoid the rashness of persecuting a movement that, if human, would fail anyway, and if divine, could not be stopped; a counsel of caution that, in the event, spared the apostles. The witnesses note Barnes's important caution: this is the mere advice of Gamaliel, who was not inspired; the principle, though containing much truth, is not an absolute or infallible rule, and should not be made a guide for all cases — for some human errors and falsehoods endure long, and some good causes are crushed for a time; the maxim true in the main, but not without exception. The expositors observe the limits of the principle as a rule: it is generally true that what is of God endures and what is of men fails; yet error sometimes flourishes long, and truth is sometimes long oppressed; so the principle is a wise observation, not a sure test of every movement at every moment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the prudence of Gamaliel's counsel, the general truth that human works fail, and the caution that this uninspired maxim is not an infallible rule. The verse thus records Gamaliel's counsel and first principle — let them alone, for a human work will fail — teaching the prudent advice to spare the apostles and the general (but not infallible) truth that merely human movements come to nothing.
39:
"But if it be of God, ye cannot overthrow it; lest haply ye be found even to fight against God." The witnesses note Gamaliel's second principle and its solemn warning. Gill notes the supposition: "if it is according to the counsel of his will, a scheme of his forming, a work to which he has called these men." Barnes observes that Gamaliel "supposed that it was at least possible that this religion was Divine," showing "a far more candid mind than the rest"; and that the arguments (the resurrection, Pentecost, the healing, the deliverance) could not but stagger the council; "ye cannot overthrow it, because God has almighty power." The witnesses note Gamaliel's second principle: if the movement be of God, it cannot be overthrown, and to oppose it is to fight against God. The witnesses note the second principle. The witnesses note the warning against fighting God.

The witnesses note Gamaliel's second principle: if of God, it cannot be overthrown — to oppose it is to fight against God.

So the witnesses make the verse Gamaliel's second principle and its warning. The lesson the expositors draw is that what is of God cannot be overthrown, and that to oppose it is to fight against God — a solemn warning. The witnesses note the supposition: "but if it be of God" — Gamaliel allows the possibility that the apostles' movement is divine; Gill notes the sense, if it is according to God's will, a work to which he has called these men. The expositors observe Barnes's point on Gamaliel's candor: he supposed it at least possible that this religion was divine — showing a more candid mind than the rest of the council; the arguments (the resurrection of Christ, the miracle of Pentecost, the healing of the lame man, the deliverance from prison) could not but stagger a thoughtful man, and Gamaliel would not dismiss the possibility of God's hand. The witnesses press the principle: "ye cannot overthrow it" — if the movement is of God, it cannot be destroyed; for God's purpose cannot be defeated, his work cannot be overthrown by men; the almighty power of God securing what is his. The expositors draw the lesson on the invincibility of God's work: what God has founded, no man can overthrow; the gospel, being of God, is invincible; persecution cannot destroy it, for it has the almighty God behind it; "if God be for us, who can be against us?" (Romans 8:31). The witnesses note the solemn warning: "lest haply ye be found even to fight against God" — to oppose a work that is of God is to fight against God himself; a fearful position, for none can prevail against the Almighty; the council warned that, in persecuting the apostles, they might be fighting God — the most dangerous and hopeless of enterprises. The expositors observe the force of this warning: if there is any possibility that the movement is divine, the council should beware, lest they be found warring against God; the prudent fear of fighting the Almighty a powerful restraint. The witnesses note the irony of Gamaliel's own position: he stated the principle that what is of God cannot be overthrown and that to oppose it is to fight God — yet did not himself embrace the gospel; he saw the danger of opposing it, but did not see (or would not confess) that it was indeed of God; cautious neutrality short of faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the invincibility of God's work, marks the warning against fighting God, and the difference between Gamaliel's caution and saving faith. The verse thus records Gamaliel's second principle — if of God, ye cannot overthrow it — teaching the invincibility of God's work and the solemn warning against fighting against God.
40:
"And to him they agreed: and when they had called the apostles, and beaten them, they commanded that they should not speak in the name of Jesus, and let them go." The witnesses note the council's partial compliance and the beating of the apostles. Barnes notes they "agreed only so far as their design of putting them to death was concerned" — they abandoned that, but "did not comply with his advice to let them entirely alone"; instead they beat them (the usual thirty-nine lashes, 2 Corinthians 11:24), as Christ had foretold (Matthew 10:17), commanded silence, and released them. The witnesses note the council, persuaded to spare the apostles' lives, yet beating them and charging them not to preach Christ. The witnesses note the partial compliance and the beating. The witnesses note the apostles scourged.

The witnesses note the council sparing the apostles' lives yet beating them — a partial, inconsistent compliance.

So the witnesses make the verse the council's partial compliance. The lesson the expositors draw is the half-measure of the council, sparing the apostles' lives yet beating and silencing them — an inconsistent compromise. The witnesses note the agreement: "to him they agreed" — the council was persuaded by Gamaliel, abandoning their design to kill the apostles; his counsel prevailing to spare their lives. The expositors observe Barnes's careful note on the partial nature of their compliance: they agreed only so far as the killing was concerned — they gave up the intent to slay; but they did not follow Gamaliel's advice to let the apostles entirely alone; instead they beat them, commanded silence, and released them. The witnesses note the beating: "when they had called the apostles, and beaten them" — the apostles were scourged (Barnes notes the usual thirty-nine lashes, 2 Corinthians 11:24), a severe and shameful punishment; the very thing Christ had foretold his disciples would suffer (Matthew 10:17, "they will scourge you"). The expositors draw the lesson on the inconsistency of the council: they spared the apostles' lives (heeding Gamaliel) yet beat them (heeding their own malice); a half-measure, neither releasing the innocent nor carrying out their murderous intent; the compromise of those convicted but unwilling to do right. The witnesses observe the persistence of their opposition: though they spared the apostles' lives, they "commanded that they should not speak in the name of Jesus" — renewing the prohibition the apostles had already defied; their malice unabated, only their method restrained; the council still set against the gospel, only checked from murder. The expositors note the fulfilment of Christ's word: the apostles' scourging fulfilled what Christ had foretold (Matthew 10:17); the disciples treated as their Master, suffering the lash for his name; the persecution Christ had promised now begun. The witnesses note the providence in the restraint: God used Gamaliel to spare the apostles' lives, while permitting the beating; the persecution bounded — the apostles afflicted but preserved, beaten but not slain; the wrath of the enemies restrained within God's appointed limits. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the half-measure of the council, the beating that fulfilled Christ's word, and the providence that spared the apostles' lives while permitting their suffering. The verse thus records the council's partial compliance — sparing their lives, beating them, commanding silence — teaching the inconsistent half-measure of the convicted council and the providence that bounded the persecution.
41:
"And they departed from the presence of the council, rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer shame for his name." The witnesses note the apostles' joy in suffering for Christ. Barnes observes that a public whipping was esteemed deeply disgraceful, and the rulers hoped the shame would deter the apostles; "yet in this they were disappointed; the effect was just the reverse." He gives the reason for their joy: they were "permitted thus to imitate the example of the Lord Jesus," who had been scourged and reviled (cf. Philippians 3:10; 1 Peter 4:13). The witnesses note the apostles leaving the council rejoicing — counting it an honor to suffer shame for the name of Christ. The witnesses note the apostles' joy. The witnesses note rejoicing in suffering.

The witnesses note the apostles rejoicing — counting it an honor to suffer shame for the name of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' joy in suffering for Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the holy joy of the apostles, who counted it an honor to suffer shame for the name of Christ. The witnesses note the response: "they departed from the presence of the council, rejoicing" — the apostles, having been beaten and threatened, left not in shame or fear, but rejoicing; their joy in the very suffering meant to crush them. The expositors observe the paradox Barnes marks: a public whipping was esteemed deeply disgraceful, and the rulers hoped the shame would deter the apostles, making them unwilling to appear in public or preach again; yet the effect was the reverse — the apostles rejoiced, the shame turned to joy. The witnesses press the ground of their joy: "that they were counted worthy to suffer shame for his name" — they counted it an honor, a privilege, to suffer for Christ's name; to be deemed worthy to share his sufferings, to bear shame for him who had borne shame for them; the suffering a badge of honor, not disgrace. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christian view of suffering for Christ: what the world counts shame, the believer counts honor; to suffer for Christ is to be counted worthy, to fellowship in his sufferings (Philippians 3:10), to share the lot of the prophets and of the Master himself; "if ye be reproached for the name of Christ, happy are ye" (1 Peter 4:14). The witnesses observe the example of the apostles' joy: they rejoiced not in the pain, but in the privilege — that they were thought worthy to suffer for so great a name; their joy a token of their love for Christ and their assurance of his approval. The expositors note the imitation of Christ: the apostles rejoiced to be permitted to imitate their Lord, who was scourged, reviled, and shamed; to suffer as he suffered, to walk the way he walked; the fellowship of his sufferings a joy to those who loved him. The witnesses draw the lesson on the transforming of shame into joy: the gospel transforms the believer's view of suffering — what the world dreads as disgrace, the saint embraces as honor; the joy of the apostles the fruit of faith and love, turning persecution into privilege. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to count it an honor to suffer shame for Christ, to rejoice in the fellowship of his sufferings, and to esteem persecution for his name a privilege, not a disgrace. The verse thus records the apostles' joy — rejoicing to suffer shame for his name — teaching the holy joy that counts suffering for Christ an honor and the privilege of fellowship in his sufferings.
42:
"And daily in the temple, and in every house, they ceased not to teach and preach Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the apostles' undeterred, constant preaching. Gill notes they taught "every day, with great constancy and assiduity, both publicly and privately; in the temple... and in each of their private houses." Clarke notes the daily temple-teaching at the hours of prayer, and presses the duty of public worship: "every man that professes Christianity should... copy their conduct." The witnesses note the apostles, undeterred by the beating and the command of silence, ceasing not to preach Jesus daily, publicly and privately — the chapter closing with the gospel's undeterred advance. The witnesses note the constant preaching. The witnesses note the undeterred apostles.

The witnesses note the apostles ceasing not to preach Jesus daily — undeterred by the beating and the command of silence.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' undeterred, constant preaching. The lesson the expositors draw is the holy boldness and constancy of the apostles, who, far from being silenced, preached Jesus the more. The witnesses note the constancy: "daily... they ceased not to teach and preach Jesus Christ" — the apostles preached every day, with great constancy and assiduity (Gill notes); not occasionally, but continually; not silenced by the council's command, but preaching the more. The expositors observe the defiance of the prohibition: the council had commanded them not to speak in the name of Jesus (v.40); yet the apostles ceased not to preach him — obeying God rather than men (v.29), continuing the very work for which they had been beaten; their boldness undeterred, their obedience to God unshaken. The witnesses note the breadth of their ministry: "in the temple, and in every house" — both publicly (in the temple, the place of public worship and concourse) and privately (in the houses, from house to house); the gospel preached in every place, openly and personally; the whole city filled with the message (as the council had complained, v.28). The expositors draw the lesson on the constancy of gospel ministry: the apostles preached daily, publicly and privately, in season and out of season (2 Timothy 4:2); their whole life given to the proclamation of Christ; a pattern of diligent, unwearied ministry. The witnesses note Clarke's pressing of the duty of public worship: the apostles' daily teaching in the temple at the hours of prayer commends the practice of public worship; every Christian should copy their conduct, attending the public worship of God; the apostles' example a rebuke to those who neglect it. The expositors observe the substance of their preaching: "to teach and preach Jesus Christ" — the apostles preached Christ, the center and substance of their message; not themselves, not abstractions, but Jesus Christ, his person, work, death, resurrection, and exaltation; Christ the theme of all their preaching. The witnesses note the triumphant close of the chapter: the chapter that began with hypocrisy judged and continued with persecution ends with the gospel undeterred, the apostles preaching Christ daily; the opposition failing, the gospel advancing; the church purified, bold, and growing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the undeterred constancy of the apostles, the boldness that defied the prohibition, the breadth of their ministry, and Christ the substance of their preaching. The verse thus records the apostles' constant preaching — ceasing not to teach and preach Jesus Christ — teaching the holy boldness and constancy that, undeterred by persecution, filled every place with the gospel of Christ.
